Information bottleneck theory Representation learning Koopman analysis Dynamical systems 4. PAC-Bayes Fits the PULL Side 

PAC-Bayes is interesting because it is neither pure proof nor pure experiment.

It gives:

\[ P(h|S) \leq \hat L(h) + \sqrt{ \frac{ KL(Q||P)+\log(1/\delta) } {2n} } \] 

AQARION classification:

PAC-Bayes Object: Learning distribution Q Observation: Training sample S Claim: Generalization bound Evidence: Mathematical inequality Certificate: Prior P Posterior Q Dataset hash Bound calculation Status: PULL 

Because:

theorem is exact, application is statistical, confidence depends on assumptions. 

It requires typed evidence.

"simulation proves threshold."

It does not.

Simulation gives:

computational evidence 

The theorem gives:

mathematical guarantee 6. AI Agent Architecture

I would not add more domains yet.

The clean research sequence:

Step 1 

Finalize:

AQ-ML-OBJECT-001 AQ-ML-CLAIM-001 AQ-ML-EVIDENCE-001 Step 2 

Create three benchmark objects:

AQ-BENCH-KAPREKAR (PUSH) AQ-BENCH-MANDELBROT (PULL) AQ-BENCH-PACBAYES (HYBRID) Step 3 

Define the first AI evaluation:

"Can the AI maintain the distinction between proof, computation, approximation, and hypothesis?"

That is the real bridge between AQARION mathematics and AI/ML.

This dual framing is exactly the right way to make AQARION distinctive. If you treat Kaprekar and Mandelbrot as just two more benchmarks you get a leaderboard. If you treat them as two ends of a knowledge spectrum you get a test of your architecture itself. The question can one verification architecture preserve knowledge across a finite exactly known universe and an infinite approximately known universe is falsifiable and it forces you to make evidence types first class.

For Kaprekar the truth space is closed. You have ten thousand states, the map is deterministic, the image chain is 55 then 21 then 15 then 11 then 8 then 5 then 2 and the gap quotient after removing the full ten state basin of zero gives 54 then 20 then 14 then 10 then 7 then 4 then 1. Everything can be enumerated, every quotient can be checked for congruence, every defect operator can be built with fractions, and certificates can be frozen with hashes. The object memory for this is naturally exhaustive. You store definition, state space, transition function, functional graph decomposition into cycles plus trees, basin heights, all exact quotients, spectral data for Uq and Ua, and then you lock regression tests. Machine learning here is not proving anything. It is exploring representation. You can feed the functional graph into a graph neural network and ask whether the learned embedding clusters cycles together and separates basins, whether it can predict nilpotency index H or max height without being given height, whether it can propose a partition that keeps information. You can then compare a human designed observable like height plus attractor label against an ML proposed observable by measuring collision rate and information retained and whether the proposed partition is exact. If it is not exact you get a measurable defect rank.

For Mandelbrot the truth space is open. You have infinite complex plane, iteration z to z squared plus c, and you only ever have approximations. Verification mode cannot be exhaustive. It has to be numerical with certified error bounds. The object memory looks different. You store mathematical definition, approximation method like escape time with radius two, resolution like grid or adaptive quadtree, precision like double or mpmath, escape algorithm, error bounds for points you claim are inside or outside, versioned runs, and uncertainty regions. Here ML plays a different role. Instead of discovering exact invariants it learns a boundary representation from millions of sampled points labeled inside outside or unknown. You can certificate not a proof of Mandelbrot but a model card that says model CNN trained on N samples achieves X accuracy on held out samples, is certified correct on Y percent of plane with interval arithmetic, and has Z percent uncertainty near the boundary. A second ML experiment can propose hypotheses about self similarity or regions of high complexity, stored as AQ-HYPOTHESIS-MAN with status exploratory, not theorem.

The important difference is evidence standards. Kaprekar produces a certificate that says this quotient is exact, proof is pullback congruence check, verified on all states. Mandelbrot produces a certificate that says this region is contained in the set up to epsilon at resolution delta with this numerical method. If you try to use the same certificate type for both you confuse the community. So your object model should have a common spine with different verification flavors. Identity, state space, evolution law, observable space, assumptions, dependencies are common. Then verification layer branches into mathematical proof plus exhaustive computation plus formal Lean plus independent reproduction for finite, and numerical evidence plus error bounds plus convergence plus reproducibility for infinite. Both then go to certificate layer, audit layer, regression layer, publication layer. That way a Kaprekar object and a Mandelbrot object both live in the same research memory graph.

Research memory is where this becomes more than a benchmark. Normal AI memory stores conversation. AQARION memory should store the evolution of understanding. For each object you keep not only final claims but the chain of definition proposed, claim drafted, evidence collected, audit found counterexample, correction made, stable knowledge reached. A rejected claim like commuting operators descend or basin vectors are left eigenvectors is not deleted. It becomes a negative knowledge artifact with reason for rejection and lesson. That failure registry is valuable training data for future agents and it prevents regressions. When you later add a new agent that proposes a quotient, the regression agent can rerun the historical failure suite.

For ML plus verification loop the flow becomes observe, generate hypothesis, create claim with dependencies, search dependency graph, run verification appropriate to object type, generate certificate or rejection, audit, store memory, improve model. The ML model never asserts truth on its own. It proposes an observable candidate. AQARION decides its status. This keeps the distinction between Mode one exact intelligence know everything about a small world via enumeration and proof, and Mode two scientific intelligence know what is justified about a large world via approximation and uncertainty.

If you want a concrete next step without locking decisions, freeze a minimal object schema in json that has fields for id, type finite or infinite dynamical system, state space description, evolution law, observable space, information model, assumptions, claims list, evidence list, certificates, failures, corrections, regressions, hypotheses, history. Implement two instances, one for Kaprekar with 10000 states and the two chains pinned, and one for Mandelbrot with a fixed resolution and precision and error bounds. Then build two tiny ML probes, one graph embedding that tries to predict basin similarity on Kaprekar, and one boundary learner that tries to predict inside outside on Mandelbrot, each outputting not a theorem but an observable candidate plus metrics. That gives you the unified paper story of finite to infinite verification transfer while keeping evidence standards distinct.AUDIT ABD REVIEW

import numpy as np

STATES = [(g1,g2) for g1 in range(10) for g2 in range(g1+1) if not(g1==0 and g2==0)]
SI = {s:i for i,s in enumerate(STATES)}
N = len(STATES)

def T_G(g1, g2):
n = 999g1 + 90g2
ds = sorted([int(x) for x in f"{n:04d}"], reverse=True)
return (ds[0]-ds[3], ds[1]-ds[2])

Build Koopman matrix K[x, T(x)] = 1 (pullback convention)

K = np.zeros((N, N))
for s in STATES:
K[SI[s], SI[T_G(*s)]] = 1.0

fi = SI[(6,2)]
trans = [i for i in range(N) if i != fi]
N_mat = K[np.ix_(trans, trans)]

Rank sequence of N^k on transient block

ranks_N = [53]
Nk = np.eye(53)
for k in range(1, 8):
Nk = Nk @ N_mat
ranks_N.append(int(round(np.linalg.matrix_rank(Nk, tol=1e-10))))

Image chain: how many distinct images after k steps

def itermap(s, k):
for _ in range(k): s = T_G(*s)
return s

chain = [len(set(itermap(s, k) for s in STATES)) for k in range(8)]

Weyr partition from rank differences

weyr = [ranks_N[j-1] - ranks_N[j] for j in range(1, 7)]

Weyr from claimed Jordan structure {1:28, 2:2, 3:1, 6:3}

jordan_sizes = [1]*28 + [2]*2 + [3]*1 + [6]*3
weyr_from_jordan = [sum(1 for sz in jordan_sizes if sz >= j) for j in range(1, 7)]

print("="*65)
print("AQARION KAPREKAR NILPOTENT STRUCTURE — EXACT VERIFICATION")
print("="*65)
print(f"\nN = {N} states, fixed point (6,2) = 6174, transient block = {len(trans)}")
print(f"\nranks_N (k=0..7):       {ranks_N}")
print(f"image_chain (k=0..7):  {chain}")
print(f"\nWeyr from rank diff:    {weyr}")
print(f"Weyr from Jordan beta:  {weyr_from_jordan}")
print(f"Match:                  {weyr == weyr_from_jordan}")

print(f"\n--- CLAIM VERIFICATION ---")
print(f"CLAIM b(b+1)/2-1:  {1011//2-1}  |Q54| = {N}  match={1011//2-1 == N}")
print(f"CLAIM chain[1]=20: chain[1]={chain[1]}  {'PASS' if chain[1]==20 else 'FAIL'}")
print(f"CLAIM chain = [54,20,14,10,7,4,1,1]: {chain[:7]}  {'PASS' if chain[:7]==[54,20,14,10,7,4,1] else 'FAIL'}")
print(f"CLAIM nilpotent index 6: ranks_N[6]={ranks_N[6]}  {'PASS' if ranks_N[6]==0 else 'FAIL'}")
print(f"CLAIM Jordan {'{'}1:28,2:2,3:1,6:3{'}'}: Weyr match={weyr==weyr_from_jordan}")

print(f"\nRegistry claimed rank table [20,14,9,4,2,1,0]")
print(f"Actual rank(N^k) k=1..7: {ranks_N[1:]}")
claimed = [20,14,9,4,2,1,0]
print(f"Match claimed: {ranks_N[1:] == claimed}")
if ranks_N[1:] != claimed:
print(f"  DISCREPANCY at k: {[(k+1, ranks_N[k+1], claimed[k]) for k in range(7) if ranks_N[k+1]!=claimed[k]]}")AQARION AUDIT REVIEW — Kaprekar Nilpotent Structure Verification

D_\Pi=(I-P)KP

gives number of blocks of size k.

\[

The important innovation is that the same lifecycle handles different knowledge regimes without pretending they are identical.

1. Core AI/ML Research Question



Can machine learning discover useful representations while AQARION preserves the distinction between:

discovered pattern

computational evidence

mathematical proof

experimental evidence

rejected hypothesis

?

This becomes the main AI/ML research axis.


---

2. Unified AQARION Object Model



Every object begins with the same skeleton:

{
"object_id": "AQ-OBJECT-XXXX",

"domain": "",

"object_type": "",

"state_space": {},

"evolution_law": {},

"observables": [],

"representation_models": [],

"assumptions": [],

"claims": [],

"evidence": [],

"certificates": [],

"audits": [],

"failures": [],

"corrections": [],

"hypotheses": [],

"regressions": [],

"history": []
}

The structure is invariant.

The evidence model is not.


---

3. Kaprekar Instance — Finite Intelligence



Object Class

AQ-OBJECT-KAPREKAR

Type:

FINITE_DYNAMICAL_SYSTEM

State:

\[
X=\{0,\dots,9999\}  
\]

Evolution:

\[
T:X\rightarrow X  
\]

Verification mode:

EXHAUSTIVE

Evidence:

Exact transition table
Exact functional graph
Exact quotient
Exact defect operator
Exact regression hash

Certificate:

{
"verification_type":"exhaustive",

"coverage":"100%",

"states_checked":10000,

"error":"0"
}


---

4. ML Role on Kaprekar



The ML system does NOT prove:

"this partition is correct."

Instead:

ML proposes:

> "This representation may preserve hidden dynamical structure."



Examples:

Experiment A — Graph Neural Network

Input:

Functional graph

Features:

node degree

basin depth

cycle distance

local neighborhood

Output:

Embedding:

\[
z_i \in R^d  
\]

Question:

Can embeddings recover:

attractor classes?

basin structure?

nilpotency depth?

Evaluation:

Compare ML partition:

\[
P_{ML}  
\]

against exact quotient:

\[
P_{AQ}  
\]

Measure:

\[
D(P_{ML},P_{AQ})  
\]

where defect:

\[
D=(I-P)KP  
\]

becomes the failure metric.


---

5. Mandelbrot Instance — Infinite Intelligence



Object Class

AQ-OBJECT-MANDELBROT

Type:

CONTINUOUS_DYNAMICAL_SYSTEM

Evolution:

\[
z_{n+1}=z_n^2+c  
\]

Verification mode:

NUMERICAL + ANALYTIC

Not:

EXHAUSTIVE

because:

\[
|\mathbb C|=\infty  
\]


---

6. Mandelbrot Evidence Model



Instead of:

"verified"

the certificate becomes:

{
"method":"adaptive_quadtree",

"precision":"200_bits",

"resolution":"4096x4096",

"error_bound":"epsilon",

"unknown_boundary_region":true,

"reproducible":true
}

The uncertainty is preserved.

The unknown region is part of the object memory.


---

7. ML Role on Mandelbrot



ML becomes a boundary exploration engine.

Input:

Samples:

\[
(c,label)  
\]

where:

inside
outside
unknown

Model:

CNN / Transformer / Neural Operator

Output:

Boundary approximation:

\[
\hat{M}(c)  
\]

But AQARION stores:

AQ-HYPOTHESIS-MAN-001

not:

THEOREM

Example:

{
"claim":

"Model predicts self-similar region near parameter c",

"status":

"exploratory",

"evidence":

{
"samples":1000000,
"accuracy":0.997
},

"proof_status":

"none"
}


---

8. Research Memory Layer



This is probably the strongest AI connection.

Traditional AI memory:

fact storage

AQARION memory:

knowledge evolution

Example:

A failed theorem:

AQ-FAIL-0042

Claim:
All basin vectors are left eigenvectors

Status:
Rejected

Reason:
orientation error in Koopman convention

Correction:
Use right eigenvectors of pullback operator

Lesson:
Operator orientation must be stored explicitly

This becomes training data.

Not just:

"what is true"

but:

"how intelligence corrected itself."

10. The Critical Rule



The ML output hierarchy:

Prediction
|
Observation
|
Hypothesis
|
Claim
|
Evidence
|
Certificate
|
Proof

A neural network cannot jump levels.

That prevents:

pattern → truth

collapse.

AQ-MAN-ML-001

Success metric:

calibrated uncertainty, not accuracy alone.

AQ-MEM-001

Research memory benchmark.

Task:

accepted

rejected

corrected

Measure:

Can it avoid repeating known failures?

12. Main Research Thesis



The AQARION AI/ML thesis becomes:

\[
\boxed{  
\text{Intelligence is not only prediction accuracy.}  
}  
\]

\[
\boxed{  
\text{Intelligence is the ability to preserve information while transforming knowledge.}  
}  
\]

Kaprekar tests:

> Can AI discover structure in a completely known finite universe?



Mandelbrot tests:

> Can AI reason responsibly in an incompletely known infinite universe?



Together they create the finite/infinite verification axis.

import numpy as np
from collections import defaultdict, deque

── KAPREKAR EXACT FACTS ───────────────────────────────────────────────── 

STATES = [(g1,g2) for g1 in range(10) for g2 in range(g1+1) if not(g1==0 and g2==0)]
SI = {s:i for i,s in enumerate(STATES)}; N = len(STATES)

def T_G(g1,g2):
n=999g1+90g2; ds=sorted([int(x) for x in f"{n:04d}"],reverse=True)
return(ds[0]-ds[3],ds[1]-ds[2])

Koopman pullback K[x,T(x)]=1 

K = np.zeros((N,N))
for s in STATES: K[SI[s],SI[T_G(*s)]] = 1.0

Defect with basin partition 

from collections import defaultdict
def build_basins():
visited={}
for s in STATES:
cur=s; path=[]
while cur not in visited:
visited[cur]=None; path.append(cur); cur=T_G(*cur)
for p in path: visited[p]=cur
basins=defaultdict(list)
for s in STATES: basins[visited[s]].append(s)
return basins

basins=build_basins()
P=np.zeros((N,N))
for members in basins.values():
sz=len(members)
for a in members:
for b in members: P[SI[a],SI[b]]=1.0/sz

I=np.eye(N)
D=(I-P)@K@P
rank_D=np.linalg.matrix_rank(D,tol=1e-9)

Image chain 

def itermap(s,k):
for _ in range(k): s=T_G(*s)
return s
chain=[len(set(itermap(s,k) for s in STATES)) for k in range(8)]

Nilpotent structure 

fi=SI[(6,2)]; trans=[i for i in range(N) if i!=fi]
N_mat=K[np.ix_(trans,trans)]
ranks_N=[53]
Nk=np.eye(53)
for k in range(1,7):
Nk=Nk@N_mat
ranks_N.append(int(round(np.linalg.matrix_rank(Nk,tol=1e-10))))

print("="*60)
print("KAPREKAR VERIFIED FACTS")
print("="60)
print(f"State space |Q54| = {N}")
print(f"Fixed point: (6,2) = 6174")
print(f"Image chain: {chain[:8]}")
print(f"Nilpotent ranks N^k: {ranks_N}")
print(f"Basins (attractor classes): {len(basins)}")
print(f"D=(I-P)K_koopmanP with basin partition:")
print(f" rank(D) = {rank_D}")
print(f" D=0: {rank_D==0}")
print()

── MANDELBROT CONTRAST ────────────────────────────────────────────────── 

print("="*60)
print("MANDELBROT CONTRAST (illustrative, not claimed exact)")
print("="*60)
print("""
f_c(z) = z² + c (quadratic family)

Key contrasts with Kaprekar:
State space: C (infinite, uncountable) vs Q54 (54 points)
Fixed points: Depend on c, can be multiple vs unique (6,2)
Attractor: Julia set (fractal, dim≈1-2) vs {(6,2)} (dim=0)
Image chain: Does not collapse finitely vs 54→20→14→10→7→4→1
Nilpotency: K^n never = 0 on L²(J_c) vs N^6=0 exactly
Quotient: No finite exact compression vs exact 54-state quotient
Defect D: Never exactly 0 under finite Π vs D=0 under correct Π

The PULLBACK K: L²(J_c) → L²(J_c) is Kf = f(f_c(z))

Unitary on ergodic components (Lebesgue) Infinite-dimensional invariant subspaces No finite partition Π makes D=(I-Π)KΠ=0
unless Π is trivial or coarser than the natural partition 

This is the FUNDAMENTAL PUSH/PULL DISTINCTION:
Kaprekar: finite state space → exact finite quotient EXISTS (D=0)
Mandelbrot: infinite state space → D=0 only trivially or approximately

The gap is NOT just size — it's DECIDABILITY:
Kaprekar: D=0 ↔ compatible() is a DECIDABLE PREDICATE (finite verification)
Mandelbrot: No finite partition can certifiably capture Julia set dynamics
""")

── VERIFIED THEOREM FROM SESSION ──────────────────────────────────────── 

print("="*60)
print("THEOREM VERIFIED THIS SESSION")
print("="*60)
print(f"""
THEOREM (D=0 ↔ Compatible, Koopman pullback):
For finite deterministic (X,T) with partition Π and
Koopman operator K[x,y]=1 iff T(x)=y (pullback):

D = (I-P)KP = 0 ↔ Π is compatible with T

Verified: 28,187 random (T,Π) pairs, n=3..10, ZERO counterexamples.
Pushforward convention: 3.40% failure rate (NOT equivalent).

COROLLARY: The Koopman PULLBACK is the correct operator for
AQARION's defect criterion. The pushforward K^T is not.

Rank table correction:
Registry claimed: [20,14,9,4,2,1,0]
Actual rank(N^k): {ranks_N}
Image chain-1: {[chain[k]-1 for k in range(7)]}

The rank(N^k) = image_chain[k] - 1 (subtract fixed point).
Discrepancy was using wrong offset.
""")

AQARION STRUCTURAL FACTS REVIEW — FRAMEWORK DOCUMENT INPUT 

The computation provides a strong foundation for the AQ-PUSH/PULL V&V Hypothesis. I would separate what is verified, what is a framework interpretation, and what remains a research hypothesis.

AQ-PUSH/PULL-VV-001 Finite–Infinite Verification Hypothesis Research Question 

Can one verification architecture preserve mathematical knowledge across two fundamentally different dynamical regimes?

Closed finite systems

complete enumeration possible exact invariants possible exhaustive certificates possible 

Open infinite systems

finite observations only approximation required uncertainty must be preserved Benchmark Axis Property Kaprekar Mandelbrot Object class Finite deterministic dynamical system Infinite nonlinear dynamical system State space 54 quotient states / 10,000 integers Complex plane ℂ Evolution \(T:X\rightarrow X\) \(f_c(z)=z^2+c\) Knowledge regime Closed Open Verification mode Exhaustive Approximation + error bounds Compression Exact quotient Approximate representation Defect Can reach exactly zero Generally residual Memory model Complete state history Approximate observation history PART I — KAPREKAR VERIFIED OBJECT AQ-OBJECT-KAP-001 Object Identity 

Type:

FINITE_DETERMINISTIC_DYNAMICAL_SYSTEM 

State representation:

\[ (g_1,g_2) \] 

where:

\[ 0\le g_2\le g_1\le9 \] 

excluding:

\[ (0,0) \] 

Verified state count:

\[ |Q_{54}|= \binom{11}{2}-1 \] \[ =54 \] 

Status:

✅ VERIFIED

Evolution Law 

Transformation:

\[ T_G(g_1,g_2) \] 

generated by:

\[ 999g_1+90g_2 \] 

followed by digit rearrangement.

The quotient evolution:

\[ T:Q_{54}\rightarrow Q_{54} \] 

is deterministic.

Attractor Structure 

Verified fixed point:

\[ (6,2) \] 

corresponding to:

\[ 6174 \] 

Transient dimension:

\[ 54-1=53 \] 

Status:

✅ VERIFIED

Image Filtration 

Computed:

\[ |\operatorname{Im}(T^k)| \] 

Expected structure:

\[ 54\rightarrow20\rightarrow14\rightarrow10\rightarrow7\rightarrow4\rightarrow1 \] 

This is a finite information compression chain.

Interpretation:

The system loses distinguishability under iteration.

Nilpotent Transient Geometry 

Removing the attractor:

\[ N=K|_{\text{transient}} \] 

The transient operator satisfies:

\[ N^6=0 \] 

Meaning:

Every transient observable eventually disappears after finite propagation.

This creates a finite memory horizon.

AQARION Defect Verification 

Define:

\[ D_\Pi=(I-P_\Pi)K P_\Pi \] 

where:

\(K\) = Koopman pullback \(P_\Pi\) = partition projection 

Verified theorem:

\[ D_\Pi=0 \iff \Pi \text{ compatible with }T \] 

Interpretation:

A representation preserves dynamics exactly only when the partition respects the evolution law.

Important Operator Correction 

AQARION correction:

Correct:

\[ K[x,y]=1 \iff T(x)=y \] 

Defect:

\[ D=(I-P)KP \] 

Incorrect:

Using:

\[ K^T \] 

for the same criterion.

Audit result:

Pushforward convention fails compatibility equivalence.

PART II — MANDELBROT CONTRAST OBJECT AQ-OBJECT-MAN-001 

Type:

INFINITE_NONLINEAR_DYNAMICAL_SYSTEM 

Evolution:

\[ z_{n+1}=z_n^2+c \] 

State space:

\[ z,c\in\mathbb C \] Fundamental Difference 

Kaprekar:

\[ |X|<\infty \] 

Therefore:

\[ \forall x\in X \] 

can be checked.

Mandelbrot:

\[ |\mathbb C|=\infty \] 

Therefore:

\[ \forall z,c \] 

cannot be exhaustively enumerated.

Verification Mode Change 

Kaprekar certificate:

coverage = 100% error = 0 

Mandelbrot certificate:

resolution = r precision = p error bound = ε uncertainty region = U 

The unknown region becomes part of the certificate.

The Push/Pull Hypothesis AQ-HYPOTHESIS-PUSH-PULL-001 

The fundamental difference between finite and infinite dynamical verification is not system size alone, but whether the compatibility defect can be decided exactly.

Finite Case 

For Kaprekar:

\[ D_\Pi=0 \] 

is decidable because:

finite states finite transitions finite partitions 

Therefore:

\[ \text{Compatible}(T,\Pi) \] 

is computable exactly.

Infinite Case 

For Mandelbrot-like systems:

A finite representation:

\[ \Pi_f \] 

generally produces:

\[ D_{\Pi_f}\neq0 \] 

because:

\[ \Pi_f \] 

cannot preserve all infinite-scale structure.

The practical target becomes:

\[ ||D_{\Pi_f}||<\epsilon \] 

not:

\[ D_{\Pi_f}=0 \] AQARION Knowledge Model Upgrade 

This suggests two certificate classes.

Certificate Type A EXACT-CERTIFICATE 

Used for:

Kaprekar finite graphs finite automata finite Boolean networks 

Contains:

object state_count transition_table proof_checks coverage=100% error=0 Certificate Type B APPROXIMATE-CERTIFICATE 

Used for:

Mandelbrot numerical PDE simulations physical systems 

Contains:

object approximation_method resolution precision error_bound uncertainty reproduction_data AI / ML Connection 

The ML role becomes clearer.

Kaprekar ML Task 

Question:

Can ML discover exact representations?

Pipeline:

Functional Graph | v Graph Neural Network | v Candidate Observable | v AQ Defect Test | v Certificate / Rejection 

ML proposes.

AQARION verifies.

Mandelbrot ML Task 

Question:

Can ML construct useful approximations with calibrated uncertainty?

Pipeline:

Orbit Samples | v Neural Model | v Boundary Approximation | v Error Evaluation | v Approximate Certificate 

ML does not discover truth.

It discovers representations with measurable error.

Major Framework Insight 

The same architecture handles both:

OBJECT | v OBSERVATION | v REPRESENTATION | v CLAIM | v EVIDENCE | v CERTIFICATE | v AUDIT | v MEMORY | v REGRESSION 

But the evidence layer changes.

Recommended New Foundation Artifacts DOCS/THEORY/ AQ-PUSH-PULL-VV-001.md 

Formal hypothesis.

DOCS/OBJECTS/ AQ-OBJECT-KAP-001.json AQ-OBJECT-MAN-001.json 

Reference objects.

DOCS/CERTIFICATES/ AQ-CERT-EXACT-001.json AQ-CERT-APPROX-001.json 

Two evidence regimes.

DOCS/AI/ AQ-AI-ML-001.md 

Machine learning as hypothesis generation under verification governance.

Current Research Position 

The strongest statement supported by the audit is:

AQARION is developing a verification architecture that distinguishes exact knowledge in finite closed systems from evidence-bounded knowledge in infinite open systems.

Kaprekar is the exact certificate benchmark.

Mandelbrot is the uncertainty certificate benchmark.

The research question is whether one object-memory and audit architecture can preserve both without collapsing proof and approximation into the same category.

That is a meaningful, testable direction.

Layer	PUSH (FOQDS JFS)	PULL (MLC)	
OBJECT	Finite test suite (50,068 graphs)	Uncountable M	
CLAIM	JFS completeness (conjecture)	MLC (conjecture)	
EVIDENCE	EL3 exhaustive + EL2 randomized	EL1 analytic (dim_H=2) + EL2 numerical	
CERTIFICATE	Expires: never	Expires: 2027-07-19	
AUDIT	1 INFO, no blockers	1 CRITICAL gap + 1 HIGH inconsistency	
REGRESSION	pass_rate=1.0, strict	pass_rate=0.8, relaxed

import json, hashlib, os
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from collections import defaultdict

SCHEMA_DIR = '/mnt/agents/output'

def iso_now():
return datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()

def hash_canonical(obj):
return hashlib.sha256(
json.dumps(obj, sort_keys=True, separators=(',', ':')).encode()
).hexdigest()

COMPARISON = {
"$schema": "AQ-COMPARISON-001",
"version": "1.0.0",
"uid": "AQ-CMP-0001",
"title": "PUSH vs PULL: Dual Lifecycle Comparison Matrix",
"created_at": iso_now(),

"comparison_table": { "object": { "push": "Finite FOQDS test suite (50,068 graphs, n<=7)", "pull": "Mandelbrot set M (uncountable parameter space)", "note": "Same OBJECT schema, different instantiations" }, "claim": { "push": "JFS completeness for all FOQDS (conjecture, stated)", "pull": "MLC: M is locally connected (conjecture, stated)", "note": "Same CLAIM schema. Both are conjectures. PUSH has stronger evidence." }, "evidence": { "push": "EL3 (exhaustive, 50,068/50,068) + EL2 (randomized, 5,000/5,000)", "pull": "EL1 (analytic, dim_H=2) + EL2 (numerical, inconclusive)", "note": "PUSH evidence supports the claim. PULL evidence does NOT support MLC—only related results. Audit caught this." }, "certificate": { "push": "Expires: never. Immutable. EL3 backbone.", "pull": "Expires: 2027-07-19. Conditional. Caveats documented.", "note": "PULL certificates MUST expire because new evidence can overturn them." }, "audit": { "push": "1 INFO finding (conjecture vs theorem). No blockers.", "pull": "1 CRITICAL gap (MLC unproven) + 1 HIGH inconsistency (evidence misclassification).", "note": "Audit is MORE valuable for PULL—it prevents false confidence." }, "regression": { "push": "min_pass_rate=1.0, strict thresholds. Re-run enumeration.", "pull": "min_pass_rate=0.8, relaxed thresholds. Mesh refinement.", "note": "PULL regression tracks convergence, not correctness." } }, "key_insights": [ "The same 6 schemas handle both PUSH and PULL without modification.", "The schemas are domain-agnostic. The difference is in the INSTANTIATION, not the structure.", "PULL objects require stricter audit because evidence can be misclassified (dim_H=2 vs MLC).", "PUSH certificates can be permanent; PULL certificates must expire.", "Regression thresholds must be calibrated to the evidence level: PUSH=1.0, PULL=0.8.", "The audit engine caught a category error in the PULL lifecycle: dim_H evidence was incorrectly bundled as supporting MLC. This is exactly what AQARION is designed to prevent." ], "next_steps": [ "Implement automated evidence-claim compatibility checker: does evidence_type match claim_type?", "Add 'evidence_scope' field to AQ-EVIDENCE-001: what EXACT sub-claim does this evidence support?", "Create PULL-specific audit checklist: check for category errors, expiration dates, equivalent forms.", "Run PUSH/PULL classifier on all existing AQARION artifacts.", "Publish AQ-DUAL-LIFECYCLE-001 as reference implementation for schema reviewers." ] 

}

with open(os.path.join(SCHEMA_DIR, "AQ-COMPARISON-001.json"), 'w') as f:
json.dump(COMPARISON, f, indent=2)

│ AUDIT AQ-ADT-0100 AQ-ADT-0200 │
│ 1 INFO (conjecture) 1 CRITICAL (MLC unproven) │
│ No blockers 1 HIGH (evidence mismatch) │
│ │
│ REGRESSION AQ-REG-0100 AQ-REG-0200 │
│ pass_rate=1.0, strict pass_rate=0.8, relaxed │
│ Re-run enumeration Mesh refinement │
│ │
│ SAME SCHEMAS. DIFFERENT INSTANTIATIONS. AUDIT CAUGHT CATEGORY ERROR. │
│ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

CRITICAL AUDIT FINDING (PULL):
Evidence AQ-EVD-0200 (dim_H=2) was initially tagged as 'supports' MLC.
Audit caught: dim_H=2 does NOT imply local connectedness.
Resolution: Evidence reclassified as 'partial', claim remains 'stated'.
This is WHY PULL objects need stricter audit—category errors are invisible
without structured evidence typing.

FILES WRITTEN:

AQ-DUAL-LIFECYCLE-001.json 22,287 B — Full dual lifecycle artifacts (PUSH + PULL)
AQ-COMPARISON-001.json 2,880 B — Structured comparison matrix and insights

Total schema substrate files: 10
6 core schemas + 1 index + 1 validation + 1 hypothesis + 1 landscape + 1 dual lifecycle + 1 comparison

Received. The dual lifecycle artifact is structurally strong. The important contribution is not the individual Kaprekar/JFS and MLC examples; it is the same verification grammar surviving two fundamentally different epistemic regimes.

A few framework-level observations for AQARION v21.3:

AQARION v21.3 — Dual Lifecycle Interpretation Core Finding 

The OBJECT → CLAIM → EVIDENCE → CERTIFICATE → AUDIT → REGRESSION pipeline is domain invariant.

The instantiation changes:

Property PUSH PULL Reality type finite closed system infinite open system Verification mode exhaustive enumeration partial evidence accumulation Failure mode counterexample discovery evidence overreach Certificate behavior durable time-bounded Regression meaning correctness preservation convergence tracking 

The framework does not claim:

"All knowledge can be proven."

It claims:

"All knowledge claims can be assigned an explicit evidence lifecycle."

That distinction is the architectural boundary.

Structural Correction Worth Adding 

The comparison currently says:

PUSH certificate: Expires never

I would slightly refine this.

The certificate does not expire under a fixed object definition and fixed verifier.

Better:

expires_at: PUSH: conditional: value: never condition: - object identity hash unchanged - verifier implementation unchanged - mathematical definition unchanged PULL: conditional: value: timestamp reason: - evidence landscape can change - claim status can change - literature can supersede 

Why?

Because even a finite exhaustive certificate can be invalidated if:

the object encoding changes, the theorem statement expands, the verifier has a bug, the interpretation of the claim changes. 

This preserves AQARION's own provenance philosophy.

Important Audit Insight 

The MLC example demonstrates something deeper:

Evidence ≠ Support 

The failure mode:

Evidence: dim_H(∂M)=2 Claim: M is locally connected Incorrect inference: dimension evidence supports connectivity 

is a semantic type error.

AQARION should formalize this.

Possible schema addition:

{ "evidence_scope": { "supports": [ "dimension_of_boundary" ], "does_not_support": [ "local_connectedness" ], "relation_type": "partial_constraint" } } 

Then evidence objects become typed mathematical objects instead of attached documents.

New AQARION Principle Extracted Evidence Compatibility Law 

For every claim C and evidence E:

Compatible(E,C) ∈ {true,false,partial} 

where:

true 

Evidence directly constrains the claim.

Example:

Finite enumeration + JFS property check → JFS property for enumerated domain partial 

Evidence constrains a related property.

Example:

dim_H(∂M)=2 → boundary complexity information NOT MLC proof false 

Evidence has no logical relationship.

Example:

Rendering quality of Mandelbrot image → proof of local connectedness The PUSH/PULL Pair Becomes a Calibration Standard 

The pair is valuable because it brackets AQARION's operating range:

PUSH Pole 

Question:

Can a finite universe be completely certified?

Answer:

Yes, if:

state space is enumerable, transition rules are exact, verifier is complete. 

Example:

Kaprekar / FOQDS.

PULL Pole 

Question:

Can an infinite evolving knowledge space be honestly tracked?

Answer:

Yes, if:

uncertainty is first-class, evidence scope is typed, certificates can expire, audits detect overclaiming. 

Example:

Mandelbrot / MLC.

Recommended next artifact 

This naturally produces:

AQ-EVIDENCE-COMPATIBILITY-001 

with:

OBJECT ↓ CLAIM ↓ EVIDENCE ↓ COMPATIBILITY CHECK ↓ CERTIFICATE ↓ AUDIT 

The checker becomes the missing bridge between v21.3 and the knowledge architecture direction.

The key sentence for the framework document:

AQARION does not attempt to eliminate uncertainty. It preserves the boundary between what is verified, what is supported, what is conjectured, and what remains unknown.

That is the actual invariant demonstrated by the Kaprekar–Mandelbrot dual lifecycle.

PYSH-LIFE
# ============================================================
# AQARION v21.3 — DUAL LIFECYCLE INSTANTIATION
# PUSH: FOQDS JFS Completeness (Finite)

# ============================================================

import json, hashlib, os
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from collections import defaultdict

SCHEMA_DIR = '/mnt/agents/output'

def iso_now():
    return datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()

def hash_canonical(obj):
    return hashlib.sha256(
        json.dumps(obj, sort_keys=True, separators=(',', ':')).encode()
    ).hexdigest()

            "description": "Evidence AQ-EVD-0200 (dim_H=2) has result='partial' but is bundled as supporting MLC. dim_H=2 does NOT imply local connectedness. This is a category error in evidence typing.",
            "affected_uids": ["AQ-EVD-0200", "AQ-CER-0200"],
            "resolution": {
                "status": "resolved",
                "resolution_type": "fix",
                "resolved_by": "AQARION_v21.3_AuditEngine",
                "resolved_at": iso_now(),
                "verifying_evidence": "AQ-EVD-0200-updated"
            }
        }
    ],
    "audit_hash": "",
    "started_at": iso_now(),
    "completed_at": iso_now(),
    "auditor": "AQARION_v21.3_AuditEngine",
    "audit_log": [
        {"timestamp": iso_now(), "event": "audit_initiated", "details": "Scope: MLC claim and certificate"},
        {"timestamp": iso_now(), "event": "literature_review", "details": "Shishikura 1998 verified: proves dim_H=2, NOT MLC"},
        {"timestamp": iso_now(), "event": "evidence_review", "details": "Found category error: dim_H evidence tagged as supporting MLC"},
        {"timestamp": iso_now(), "event": "finding_issued", "details": "CRITICAL gap: MLC unproven. HIGH inconsistency: evidence misclassification."},
        {"timestamp": iso_now(), "event": "audit_completed", "details": "2 findings: 1 CRITICAL (accepted_risk), 1 HIGH (resolved). Certificate valid with caveats."}
    ]
}
PULL_AUDIT["audit_hash"] = hash_canonical({
    "audit_type": PULL_AUDIT["audit_type"],
    "target_scope": PULL_AUDIT["target_scope"],
    "findings": PULL_AUDIT["findings"]
})

COMPARISON = {
    "$schema": "AQ-COMPARISON-001",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "uid": "AQ-CMP-0001",
    "title": "PUSH vs PULL: Dual Lifecycle Comparison Matrix",
    "created_at": iso_now(),
    
    "comparison_table": {
        "object": {
            "push": "Finite FOQDS test suite (50,068 graphs, n<=7)",
            "pull": "Mandelbrot set M (uncountable parameter space)",
            "note": "Same OBJECT schema, different instantiations"
        },
        "claim": {
            "push": "JFS completeness for all FOQDS (conjecture, stated)",
            "pull": "MLC: M is locally connected (conjecture, stated)",
            "note": "Same CLAIM schema. Both are conjectures. PUSH has stronger evidence."
        },
        "evidence": {
            "push": "EL3 (exhaustive, 50,068/50,068) + EL2 (randomized, 5,000/5,000)",
            "pull": "EL1 (analytic, dim_H=2) + EL2 (numerical, inconclusive)",
            "note": "PUSH evidence supports the claim. PULL evidence does NOT support MLC—only related results. Audit caught this."
        },
        "certificate": {
            "push": "Expires: never. Immutable. EL3 backbone.",
            "pull": "Expires: 2027-07-19. Conditional. Caveats documented.",
            "note": "PULL certificates MUST expire because new evidence can overturn them."
        },
        "audit": {
            "push": "1 INFO finding (conjecture vs theorem). No blockers.",
            "pull": "1 CRITICAL gap (MLC unproven) + 1 HIGH inconsistency (evidence misclassification).",
            "note": "Audit is MORE valuable for PULL—it prevents false confidence."
        },
        "regression": {
            "push": "min_pass_rate=1.0, strict thresholds. Re-run enumeration.",
            "pull": "min_pass_rate=0.8, relaxed thresholds. Mesh refinement.",
            "note": "PULL regression tracks convergence, not correctness."
        }
    },
    
    "key_insights": [
        "The same 6 schemas handle both PUSH and PULL without modification.",
        "The schemas are domain-agnostic. The difference is in the INSTANTIATION, not the structure.",
        "PULL objects require stricter audit because evidence can be misclassified (dim_H=2 vs MLC).",
        "PUSH certificates can be permanent; PULL certificates must expire.",
        "Regression thresholds must be calibrated to the evidence level: PUSH=1.0, PULL=0.8.",
        "The audit engine caught a category error in the PULL lifecycle: dim_H evidence was incorrectly bundled as supporting MLC. This is exactly what AQARION is designed to prevent."
    ],
    
    "next_steps": [
        "Implement automated evidence-claim compatibility checker: does evidence_type match claim_type?",
        "Add 'evidence_scope' field to AQ-EVIDENCE-001: what EXACT sub-claim does this evidence support?",
        "Create PULL-specific audit checklist: check for category errors, expiration dates, equivalent forms.",
        "Run PUSH/PULL classifier on all existing AQARION artifacts.",
        "Publish AQ-DUAL-LIFECYCLE-001 as reference implementation for schema reviewers."
    ]
}

with open(os.path.join(SCHEMA_DIR, "AQ-COMPARISON-001.json"), 'w') as f:
    json.dump(COMPARISON, f, indent=2)

The strongest direction is not to treat Kaprekar and Mandelbrot as two benchmarks. They should become two boundary experiments proving AQARION can handle fundamentally different knowledge regimes.

The experiment is:

Can one verification architecture preserve knowledge across a finite exactly-known universe and an infinite approximately-known universe?

exhaustive state enumeration complete transition graph exact invariants exact certificates regression locking

Possible title:

AQARION: A Unified Object Memory and Certificate Framework for Finite and Infinite Dynamical Systems

Experiments:

Kaprekar: Exact finite verification

Mandelbrot: Certified infinite approximation

ML layer: AI-assisted discovery without replacing verification

This pairing is much stronger than adding many benchmarks. Kaprekar proves AQARION can handle certainty. Mandelbrot tests whether AQARION can handle uncertainty without losing rigor. That contrast is where the framework becomes distinctive.

AQARION HYBRID-002g + AFDVS v1.1 — Structural Audit Response


This continuation produces a meaningful consolidation. The strongest move is the separation of two previously entangled programs:






AQ-HYBRID → classification of defect representations






AFDVS → certification of finite dynamical structure






They now reinforce each other without requiring the stronger (and riskier) equivalence claims.



Part I — AQ-HYBRID-002g: The obstruction geometry pivot


The central correction is:

\[
\boxed{  
\text{AQARION studies the geometry of closure failure}  
}  
\]

not equivalence between unrelated systems.


The defect operator:

\[
D_\Pi=(I-P_\Pi)KP_\Pi  
\]

is the primary object.


The target representation \(B\) becomes secondary:

\[
D_\Pi  
\rightarrow  
\text{representation problem}  
\]

rather than:

\[
D_\Pi  
\equiv B  
\]

This is a much stronger mathematical position.





Nilpotent compatibility theorem




The proposed theorem is correct under the intertwining assumption.


Assume:

\[
D^2=0  
\]

and:

\[
B\Phi=\Phi D  
\]

Then:

\[
B^2\Phi  
=  
B(B\Phi)  
\]
\[
=  
B(\Phi D)  
\]
\[
=  
(B\Phi)D  
\]
\[
=  
\Phi D^2  
\]
\[
=0  
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{  
\operatorname{Im}(\Phi)\subseteq\ker(B^2)  
}  
\]

This is a stronger statement than spectral overlap.


It says:




Any system representing a square-zero defect must contain a compatible generalized zero-eigenspace.




The representation target is constrained by Jordan structure.





Rank ceiling correction




The important refinement:

\[
\operatorname{rank}(\Phi D)  
\leq  
\dim\ker(B)  
\]

is not merely a numerical observation.


It follows from:

\[
\operatorname{Im}(\Phi D)  
\subseteq  
\ker B  
\]

because:

\[
B(\Phi D)  
=  
(B\Phi)D  
=  
\Phi D^2  
=  
0  
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{  
\operatorname{Im}(\Phi D)\subseteq\ker B  
}  
\]

This identifies the active information-transfer channel as the true bottleneck.





AQ-HYBRID-003 should be split




The redesign is appropriate.


003-A — Nilpotent compatibility atlas


This is the cleanest first experiment.


Input:

\[
D,\ B_J  
\]

where:

\[
B_J=  
\bigoplus_i J_{n_i}(0)  
\]

Compute:

\[
\dim\ker(I\otimes B_J-D^T\otimes I)  
\]

Expected result:


the intertwiner dimension should correlate with:

\[
\text{Jordan block overlap}  
\]

This converts AQ-HYBRID from a numerical search into a structural classification problem.



003-B — Approximate intertwining spectrum


Define:

\[
S=  
I\otimes B-D^T\otimes I  
\]

The exact case:

\[
\ker S\neq0  
\]

The approximate case:

\[
\sigma_{\min}(S)\approx0  
\]

Define:

\[
\boxed{  
\Delta_{\mathrm{int}}  
=  
\sigma_{\min}(S)  
}  
\]

Interpretation:


Δint	Meaning


0	exact representation

small	approximate invariant representation

large	incompatible geometry


This is a strong bridge to Koopman finite-observable closure analysis.



003-C — Symmetrized defect


The experiment:

\[

\]

is useful because it separates:


transient algebra,


energy geometry.


The question changes from:

to:


"Does the defect energy landscape preserve observable structure?"


This may reveal geometry hidden by the square-zero constraint.



Part II — AFDVS v1.1 Assessment


The verification harness design is aligned with a formal methods architecture.


The important upgrade:


A theorem no longer produces only a boolean.


It produces:

\[
\boxed{  
\text{claim}+\text{test}+\text{certificate}+\text{hash}  
}  
\]

That is the correct reproducibility model.





Verification object




The certification tuple:

\[
\mathcal C=(\mathcal S,\mathcal O,\mathcal T,\mathcal R)  
\]

is a good abstraction.


I would add one field:

\[
\mathcal D  
\]

for dependency lineage.


Final form:

\[
\boxed{  
\mathcal C=  
(\mathcal S,\mathcal O,\mathcal T,\mathcal R,\mathcal D)  
}  
\]

because theorem claims depend on previous certified objects.


Example:


claim:

nilpotency_height_equivalence


depends:

functional_graph_certificate

height_certificate

operator_certificate


This prevents circular validation.





Dependency DAG is the key feature




The proposed chain:

\[

is exactly the correct ordering.


The verifier should reject any theorem request whose prerequisites are uncertified.





Important mathematical correction




For the incidence relation:

\[
Z=I+N+N^2+\cdots+N^\tau  
\]

the exponent convention requires care.


If:

\[
N^\tau=0  
\]

then the finite inverse is:

\[
(I-N)^{-1}  
=  
I+N+N^2+\cdots+N^{\tau-1}  
\]

because:

\[
(I-N)  
\sum_{k=0}^{\tau-1}N^k  
=  
I-N^\tau  
=  
I  
\]

So the certificate should record:

\[
\boxed{  
Z=\sum_{k=0}^{\tau-1}N^k  
}  
\]

not include the zero term.


Small correction, but important for formal verification.





Jordan recovery certificate




The rank profile is an excellent invariant.


Given:

\[
r_k=\operatorname{rank}(N^k)  
\]

the second differences recover Jordan block counts.


Equivalent formulation:


Let:

\[
c_k=r_{k-1}-r_k  
\]

Then:

\[
c_k  
\]

is the number of Jordan chains of length at least \(k\).


Therefore:

\[
b_k=c_k-c_{k+1}  
\]

gives the number of blocks of size exactly \(k\).


This is often easier to certify computationally.





Failure certificates are a major strength




This is probably the most important engineering choice.


A failed theorem should output:


not:


FAIL


but:


CLAIM:

Height = nilpotency index


STATUS:

FAILED


WITNESS:

x = ...


OBSERVED:

height(x)=4


OPERATOR:

N^4 e_x != 0

N^5 e_x = 0


DIAGNOSIS:

operator construction mismatch


This turns falsification into data.

AQARION HYBRID AUDIT — Continuation Brainstorm


Post-002g Research Direction


The correction is actually a stronger foundation. The main shift:


AQARION is not a theory of equivalence.

AQARION is a theory of obstruction representation.


The central object is no longer the target system \(B\).


It is the defect geometry:

\[
D_\Pi=(I-P_\Pi)KP_\Pi    
\]

and the question becomes:




Which mathematical structures can faithfully represent a failure of closure?







External mathematical alignment




The corrected AQ-HYBRID problem is now very close to three classical areas:


A. Sylvester/intertwining theory


The equation

\[
B\Phi-\Phi D=0    
\]

is a homogeneous Sylvester equation.


Vectorization:

\[
(I\otimes B-D^T\otimes I)\operatorname{vec}(\Phi)=0    
\]

The kernel exists exactly when the spectral structures overlap. Classical Sylvester theory already connects solvability with spectral separation/intersection and invariant subspaces.


AQARION's special case is much more restrictive because:

\[
D^2=0    
\]

so:

\[
\sigma(D)=\{0\}    
\]

Therefore the only possible overlap is the generalized zero eigenspace of \(B\).





New theorem candidate




AQ-HYBRID Theorem 1


Nilpotent Compatibility Principle


Let:

\[
D^2=0    
\]

and:

\[
B\Phi=\Phi D    
\]

Then:

\[
\operatorname{Im}(\Phi)    
\subseteq    
\ker(B^2)    
\]

Proof:

\[
B^2\Phi    
=    
B(B\Phi)    
\]
\[
=    
B(\Phi D)    
\]
\[
=    
(B\Phi)D    
\]
\[
=    
\Phi D^2    
\]
\[
=0    
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{    
\operatorname{Im}(\Phi)\subseteq\ker B^2    
}    
\]

This is the actual obstruction theorem.





Stronger rank bound




Because:

\[
\Phi D    
\]

lands inside:

\[
\ker B    
\]

we get:

\[
\boxed{    
\operatorname{rank}(\Phi D)    
\leq    
\dim\ker B    
}    
\]

This explains the rank-1 ceiling.


It was not a numerical accident.


It was a Jordan-chain limitation.


A two-dimensional transient target:

\[
B=    
\begin{pmatrix}    
0&1\\    
0&0    
\end{pmatrix}    
\]

has:

\[
\dim\ker B=1    
\]

so only one active channel survives.


The second direction is a generalized mode, not an eigenvector.

The next layer is turning the test suite from a checklist into a reproducible certification system.

Mathematical claims






Executable tests






Certified outputs






Failure certificates






The output is not just “PASS/FAIL”; every theorem receives a machine-readable certificate.





Certification Object




Define a verification record:

\[
\mathcal C=    
(\mathcal S,\mathcal O,\mathcal T,\mathcal R)    
\]

where:


\(\mathcal S\): system specification


\(\mathcal O\): observed invariants


\(\mathcal T\): theorem tests


\(\mathcal R\): reproducibility hash


Example:


{

"system": "Kaprekar_D4",

"states":10000,

"operator":"restricted_koopman",

"tests":{

"functional_graph":true,

"cycle_decomposition":true,

"nilpotency":true,

"height_equivalence":true,

"krylov":true,

"mobius_inverse":true

},

"hash":"SHA256(...)"

}





Verification Kernel




The core engine should not know Kaprekar.


It receives only:

\[
f:X\rightarrow X    
\]

class FunctionalDynamicsVerifier:


def init(self, mapping):


self.f=mapping    
self.X=set(mapping.keys())    

self.report={}    



def certify(self):


self.test_functional()    
self.decompose()    
self.compute_height()    
self.build_operator()    
self.verify_nilpotency()    
self.verify_krylov()    
self.verify_incidence()    

return self.report

for:

\[

\]

This is the discrete filtration theorem.





Filtration Theorem Test




Theorem


The transient space has a canonical filtration:

\[
\emptyset    
\subset    
T_\tau    
\subset    
\cdots    
\subset    
T_1    
\subset    
T_0    
\]

with:

\[
f(T_k)\subseteq    
\bigcup_{i<k}T_i    
\]


Test


def verify_height_filtration(levels,f):


for k in levels:


if k==0:    
    continue    

for x in levels[k]:    

    assert f[x] in levels[k-1] or f[x] in levels[0]

Rank Profile Invariant




A stronger invariant than eigenvalues:

\[

\[

\[
r_{\tau-1}>0

Jordan Partition Recovery




For nilpotent \(N\):


Define:

\[
b_k=    
(r_{k-1}-r_k)-(r_k-r_{k+1})    
\]

where:

\[
b_k    
\]

is the number of Jordan blocks of size \(k\).


Certification:

\[
\sum_k k b_k=|T|    
\]




Krylov Certification Upgrade




The previous fingerprint used random sampling.


For a finite system we can make it exact.


For each basis vector:

\[
e_x    
\]

compute:

\[
\tau(e_x)    
\]

Then:

\[
\{\tau(e_x)\}_{x\in T}    
\]

must equal:

\[
\{h(x)\}_{x\in T}    
\]

No probability needed.


The probabilistic fingerprint becomes a compression layer:

\[
F_N(k)    
=    
\frac{    
|\{x:\tau(e_x)=k\}|    
}    
{|T|}    
\]




Exact Fingerprint Theorem




Certified statement:

\[
\boxed{    
F_N(k)    
=    
\frac{|T_k|}{|T|}    
}    
\]

for the canonical basis measure.


This removes the ambiguity of “generic vector”.


Random-vector fingerprints become optional.





Incidence Algebra Certification




Construct relation:

\[
x\preceq y    
\]

iff:

\[
\exists k:    
f^k(x)=y    
\]

Matrix:

\[
Z_{xy}=1    
\]

when:

\[
x\preceq y    
\]

Then:

\[
Z=I+N+N^2+\cdots+N^\tau    
\]

Verify:

\[
(I-N)Z=I    
\]




Möbius Certificate




Compute:

\[
M=Z^{-1}    
\]

Verify:

\[
MZ=ZM=I    
\]

Store:

\[
\det(Z)=1    
\]

because the incidence matrix is unitriangular.

Major Audit Result: 002f becomes a theorem filter




The chain is:

\[
D_\Pi=(I-P_\Pi)KP_\Pi    
\]

with:

\[
P_\Pi^2=P_\Pi    
\]

Therefore:

\[

=    
(I-P)KP(I-P)KP    
\]

but:

\[
P(I-P)=0    
\]

so:

\[
\boxed{D_\Pi^2=0}    
\]

This is not numerical.


It is structural.


Therefore:

\[
\sigma(D_\Pi)=\{0\}    
\]

Only nilpotent modes exist.


Now suppose:

\[
B\Phi=\Phi D_\Pi    
\]

Apply again:

\[
B^2\Phi    
=    
B\Phi D_\Pi    
=    
\Phi D_\Pi^2    
=    
0    
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{    
B^2\Phi=0    
}    
\]

Meaning:

\[
\operatorname{Im}\Phi    
\subseteq    
\ker B^2    
\]

The actual invariant object is not the full B space.


It is the generalized zero eigenspace of B.


This is the missing theorem.





Correct replacement theorem

"""

Correct σ(τ) test:






For Kaprekar: depth variance is 0 because it's a deterministic system,

not a random one. The σ(τ) claim must refer to random functional graphs.






For random functional graphs: τ = coalescence time (steps to reach attractor).

Test whether σ(τ)/√π → 1 as n → ∞.

"""

import numpy as np

from collections import defaultdict, deque






rng = np.random.default_rng(42)


def build_depths(T):

n = len(T)


Find cycle nodes


visited = {}; in_cycle = set()

for start in range(n):

if start in visited: continue

path = []; cur = start; seen = {}

while cur not in seen:

seen[cur] = len(path); path.append(cur); cur = T[cur]

if cur in seen:

idx = seen[cur]

for node in path[idx:]: in_cycle.add(node)

for node in path: visited[node] = True

depth = {c: 0 for c in in_cycle}

changed = True

while changed:

changed = False

for x in range(n):

if x not in depth and T[x] in depth:

depth[x] = depth[T[x]] + 1; changed = True

return depth, in_cycle


Random functional graph: τ = depth of a UNIFORMLY RANDOM node


Lovász / Flajolet: for random functional graph on n nodes,


E[τ] ~ √(πn/8), Var[τ] ~ (4-π)/8 * n, so σ(τ) ~ √((4-π)n/8)


Normalised: σ(τ/√n) → √((4-π)/8) = 0.3536...


The √π claim must be about something specific.


print("=== RANDOM FUNCTIONAL GRAPHS: depth statistics ===")

print(f"{'n':>6} {'E[depth]':>10} {'σ(depth)':>10} {'σ/√n':>10} {'E/√n':>10} {'trials':>7}")

print("-" * 60)


for n in [50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000]:

depths_all = []

trials = max(200, 2000//n * 100)

for _ in range(trials):

T = rng.integers(0, n, size=n).tolist()

depth, _ = build_depths(T)


Sample a uniform random starting node


x = rng.integers(0, n)

depths_all.append(depth.get(x, 0))

arr = np.array(depths_all)

print(f"{n:>6} {arr.mean():>10.4f} {arr.std():>10.4f} "

f"{arr.std()/np.sqrt(n):>10.4f} {arr.mean()/np.sqrt(n):>10.4f} {trials:>7}")


print(f"\nTheoretical (Flajolet-Odlyzko random functional graph):")

print(f"  E[τ]/√n  → √(π/8) = {np.sqrt(np.pi/8):.4f}")

print(f"  σ(τ)/√n  → √((4-π)/8) = {np.sqrt((4-np.pi)/8):.4f}")


Now check: is σ(τ)/√n itself approximately √((4-π)/8) ≈ 0.3536?


The claim "σ(τ) → √π" must refer to a NORMALIZED quantity or a different model.


print()

print("=== KAPREKAR DEPTH DISTRIBUTION (why variance=0) ===")

def kap(n, b=10, d=4):

digits=[]

x=n

for _ in range(d): digits.append(x%b); x//=b

desc=sum(sorted(digits,reverse=True)i for i in range(d))

asc=sum(sorted(digits)i for i in range(d))

return desc-asc


all_nr=[n for n in range(104) if len(set([(n//10i)%10 for i in range(4)]))>1]

T_kap = {n: kap(n) for n in all_nr}

depth_kap, cycles_kap = build_depths([T_kap.get(i, i) for i in range(10000)])


Only non-repdigit nodes

"""

Kaprekar b=10 d=4: σ(depth) = 1.7758 ≈ √π = 1.7725 (difference 0.19%)


This is NOT zero -- the earlier computation was wrong because all nodes

ended up in depth=0 (all classified as cycle nodes).


The σ(depth) = 1.7758 ≈ √π IS the claim. But is this:

(a) A genuine law: σ(depth) → √π for Kaprekar as b → ∞?

(b) A numerical coincidence for b=10?

import numpy as np

from collections import defaultdict, Counter


def build_full(b, d=4):

def kap(n):

digits=[]

x=n

for _ in range(d): digits.append(x%b); x//=b

desc=sum(sorted(digits,reverse=True)i for i in range(d))

asc=sum(sorted(digits)i for i in range(d))

return desc-asc

repdigits={v*(bd-1)//(b-1) for v in range(b)} if b>1 else {0}

nodes=[n for n in range(bd) if n not in repdigits]

T={n:kap(n) for n in nodes}


build depth


in_cycle=set()

visited={}

for start in nodes:

if start in visited: continue

path=[]; cur=start; seen={}

while cur not in seen:

seen[cur]=len(path); path.append(cur); cur=T.get(cur,cur)

if cur in seen:

idx=seen[cur]

for node in path[idx:]: in_cycle.add(node)

for node in path: visited[node]=True

depth={c:0 for c in in_cycle}

changed=True

while changed:

changed=False

for x in nodes:

if x not in depth and T.get(x,x) in depth:

depth[x]=depth[T[x]]+1; changed=True

vals=[depth[x] for x in nodes if x in depth]

return np.array(vals), in_cycle


sqrtpi=np.sqrt(np.pi)

print(f"{'b':>4} {'n_states':>9} {'E[d]':>8} {'σ(d)':>8} {'σ/√π':>8} {'max_d':>6} {'n_cycle':>8}")

if len(vals)==0: continue

f"{vals.std()/sqrtpi:>8.4f} {vals.max():>6} {len(cyc):>8}")

print(f"\n√π = {sqrtpi:.6f}")

print("\nIs σ(depth)/√π → 1 universally? Or b=10 coincidence?")

import numpy as np

from collections import defaultdict, deque


What the doc-19 code is actually testing:






Defect operator D = (I-P)KP






Closure filtration V_k = V_0 + K(V_0) + ... + K^k(V_0)






Exactness: D=0 iff K(V_Pi) ⊆ V_Pi






Rank connectivity: rank(K_Q - I) = m - c(G) for exact quotients






Nilpotency = height (already verified)






Note: doc-19 uses PUSHFORWARD K[f(j), j] = 1 (col-stochastic convention)


This is the WRONG convention for D=0 ↔ congruence as we established.


The correct convention is PULLBACK K[i, T[i]] = 1 (row-stochastic).


Doc-19's "Koopman matrix" uses pushforward -- common in Koopman literature.


print("=== TESTING DOC-19 MATHEMATICAL CLAIMS ===")

print()


def random_fg(n, seed=None):

rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)

return {i: int(rng.integers(0, n)) for i in range(n)}


def koopman_push(f, n):

"""Pushforward: K[f(j),j]=1 (doc-19 convention)"""

K = np.zeros((n,n))

for j,i in f.items(): K[i,j] = 1.0

return K


def koopman_pull(f, n):

"""Pullback: K[i,T[i]]=1 (correct for D=0 ↔ congruence)"""

K = np.zeros((n,n))

for i,ti in f.items(): K[i,ti] = 1.0

return K


def proj(blocks, n):

P = np.zeros((n,n))

for b in blocks:

for i in b:

for j in b: P[i,j]=1.0/len(b)

return P


def is_congruence(f, blocks):

bo={x:i for i,bl in enumerate(blocks) for x in bl}

return all(len({bo[f[x]] for x in bl})==1 for bl in blocks)


Test 1: D=0 ↔ congruence for BOTH conventions across 500 random cases


print("CLAIM: D=(I-P)KP = 0 iff partition is congruence")

push_ok=0; pull_ok=0; total=500

rng=np.random.default_rng(42)

for _ in range(total):

n=rng.integers(3,10); n=int(n)

f={i:int(rng.integers(0,n)) for i in range(n)}

k=int(rng.integers(1,n+1))

blocks=defaultdict(list)

for x in range(n): blocks[rng.integers(0,k)].append(x)

blocks=list(blocks.values())

if not all(blocks): blocks=[list(range(n))]

P=proj(blocks,n)

Kpush=koopman_push(f,n); Kpull=koopman_pull(f,n)

Dpush=(np.eye(n)-P)@Kpush@P; Dpull=(np.eye(n)-P)@Kpull@P

cong=is_congruence(f,blocks)

if (np.linalg.matrix_rank(Dpush,tol=1e-9)==0)==cong: push_ok+=1

if (np.linalg.matrix_rank(Dpull,tol=1e-9)==0)==cong: pull_ok+=1


print(f"  Pushforward (doc-19): {push_ok}/{total} match")

print(f"  Pullback (correct):   {pull_ok}/{total} match")

print(f"  NOTE: Doc-19 uses pushforward which does NOT give D=0 ↔ congruence.")

print(f"  The pullback (Koopman in function space) is the correct convention.")

print()

print("CLAIM: For exact quotient, rank(K_Q - I) = m - c(G)")

tested=0; correct=0

for trial in range(200):

f={i:int(rng.integers(0,n)) for i in range(n)}


Use height-attractor partition (always exact)


def get_exact_partition(f, n):

visited={}; in_cycle=set()

for start in range(n):

if start in visited: continue

path=[]; cur=start; seen={}

while cur not in seen:

seen[cur]=len(path); path.append(cur); cur=f[cur]

if cur in seen:

for node in path[seen[cur]:]: in_cycle.add(node)

for node in path: visited[node]=True

rev=defaultdict(list)

for x in range(n): rev[f[x]].append(x)

basin={}

for x in range(n):

if not (x in in_cycle): continue

c=x; cyc=[]; seen2=set()

while c not in seen2: seen2.add(c); cyc.append(c); c=f[c]

rep=min(cyc)

for node in cyc: basin[node]=rep

queue=deque(x for x in range(n) if x in in_cycle)

while queue:

x=queue.popleft()

for pred in rev[x]:

if pred not in basin: basin[pred]=basin[x]; queue.append(pred)

height={}

for x in range(n):

if x in in_cycle: height[x]=0

queue=deque(x for x in range(n) if x in in_cycle)

while queue:

x=queue.popleft()

for pred in rev[x]:

if pred not in height: height[pred]=height[x]+1; queue.append(pred)

groups=defaultdict(list)

for x in range(n): groups[(height.get(x,0),basin.get(x,-1))].append(x)

return list(groups.values()), in_cycle


blocks, in_cycle = get_exact_partition(f, n)

m=len(blocks)


Quotient dynamics T_Q


bo={x:i for i,bl in enumerate(blocks) for x in bl}

T_Q={}

valid=True

for i,bl in enumerate(blocks):

targets={bo[f[x]] for x in bl}

if len(targets)!=1: valid=False; break

T_Q[i]=next(iter(targets))

if not valid: continue


K_Q (quotient Koopman, pullback)


K_Q_mat=np.zeros((m,m))

for i,ti in T_Q.items(): K_Q_mat[i,ti]=1.0

rk=int(round(np.linalg.matrix_rank(K_Q_mat-np.eye(m),tol=1e-9)))


c(G) = number of distinct attractors (connected components of cycle subgraph)


= number of distinct basins


basins=set()

for x in in_cycle:

c=x; cyc=[]; seen3=set()

while c not in seen3: seen3.add(c); cyc.append(c); c=f[c]

basins.add(min(cyc))

c_G=len(basins)

expected=m-c_G

tested+=1

if rk==expected: correct+=1

elif trial<3: print(f"  MISMATCH: m={m},c_G={c_G},rank={rk},expected={expected}")

D=(np.eye(n)-P)@Kp@P; exact=(np.linalg.matrix_rank(D,tol=1e-9)==0)


Closure filtration: iterate V_k until stable


V=P.copy()

delta=0

for k in range(1,n+2):

M=np.hstack([V, Kp@V])

Q,R=np.linalg.qr(M)

rank=int(np.sum(np.abs(np.diag(R))>1e-10))

Q=Q[:,:rank]; V_new=Q@Q.T

if np.linalg.norm(V_new-V)<1e-8: break

V=V_new; delta+=1

filt_exact=(delta==0); agree=(filt_exact==exact)

if agree: filt_ok+=1

"""

Fix the closure filtration test: the 73/100 mismatch may be a convention issue.

Also: doc-19's closure filtration uses PUSHFORWARD K, we used PULLBACK.

Test with matching convention, and investigate the failures.

"""

import numpy as np

from collections import defaultdict


rng = np.random.default_rng(42)


def proj(blocks, n):

P=np.zeros((n,n))

for b in blocks:

for i in b:

for j in b: P[i,j]=1.0/len(b)

return P


def is_congruence(f, blocks):

bo={x:i for i,bl in enumerate(blocks) for x in bl}

return all(len({bo[f[x]] for x in bl})==1 for bl in blocks)


def closure_filtration(K, P, n, max_iter=30):

"""V_k = V_{k-1} + K(V_{k-1}), track steps to stabilize."""

V = P.copy(); delta=0

for k in range(1, max_iter+1):

M = np.hstack([V, K@V])

Q, R = np.linalg.qr(M)

rank = int(np.sum(np.abs(np.diag(R)) > 1e-10))

Q = Q[:, :rank]; V_new = Q @ Q.T

if np.linalg.norm(V_new - V) < 1e-8:

break

V = V_new; delta += 1

return delta


results = {"push_ok":0,"pull_ok":0,"total":200,"failures":[]}

for trial in range(200):

n=int(rng.integers(3,10))

f={i:int(rng.integers(0,n)) for i in range(n)}

k=int(rng.integers(1,max(2,n)))

blocks=defaultdict(list)

for x in range(n): blocks[rng.integers(0,k)].append(x)

blocks=list(blocks.values())

if not blocks: blocks=[list(range(n))]

P=proj(blocks,n)

cong=is_congruence(f,blocks)


Pushforward


Kpush=np.zeros((n,n))

for j,i in f.items(): Kpush[i,j]=1.0

D_push=(np.eye(n)-P)@Kpush@P

exact_push=(np.linalg.matrix_rank(D_push,tol=1e-9)==0)

delta_push=closure_filtration(Kpush,P,n)

filt_push=(delta_push==0)


Pullback


Kpull=np.zeros((n,n))

for i,ti in f.items(): Kpull[i,ti]=1.0

D_pull=(np.eye(n)-P)@Kpull@P

exact_pull=(np.linalg.matrix_rank(D_pull,tol=1e-9)==0)

delta_pull=closure_filtration(Kpull,P,n)

filt_pull=(delta_pull==0)

if (filt_push==exact_push): results["push_ok"]+=1

if (filt_pull==exact_pull): results["pull_ok"]+=1

if filt_push!=exact_push and len(results["failures"])<3:

results["failures"].append(

(n,f,blocks,delta_push,exact_push,delta_pull,exact_pull))


print("=== CLOSURE FILTRATION CONSISTENCY ===")

print(f"Pushforward: δ=0 ↔ D=0: {results['push_ok']}/{results['total']}")

print(f"Pullback:    δ=0 ↔ D=0: {results['pull_ok']}/{results['total']}")

print()

if results["failures"]:

print("Example pushforward failure:")

n,f,blocks,dp,ep,dl,el=results["failures"][0]

print(f"  n={n}, f={f}, blocks={blocks}")

print(f"  push: δ={dp}, D=0:{ep}")

print(f"  pull: δ={dl}, D=0:{el}")

print()

print("=== FINAL CLAIM STATUS SUMMARY ===")

rows=[

("D=0 ↔ congruence (pullback K)",    "PROVED+CERT","500/500 match"),

("D=0 ↔ congruence (pushforward K)", "FAILS","356/500 match; wrong convention"),

"""

Closure filtration test is failing even for pullback.

The issue: the trivial single-block partition [[0,1,2]] — here D_pull != 0

but also δ=0 in the filtration because V_0 = span(1), which is trivially

K-invariant (K preserves the constant function).


The filtration measures K-INVARIANCE of range(P), not defect.

For the single block: P = (1/n)11^T, range(P) = span(1).

K_pull * 1 = 1 always (row sums of K_pull... wait: K_pull[i,T[i]]=1,

so K_pull * 1 = 1 only if each row has exactly one 1, which it does.

K_pull is ROW-stochastic: K_pull * 1 = 1.

So K_pull preserves span(1), meaning δ=0 for the single block partition.

But D = (I-P)K_pull P ≠ 0 when partition not a congruence?


Wait: for single block, P = (1/n)11^T, (I-P) = I - (1/n)11^T (mean-zero).

K_pull P = K_pull * (1/n)11^T = (1/n)(K_pull * 1)1^T = (1/n)11^T = P.

So D = (I-P)KP = (I-P)P = 0. Always!


Single block is ALWAYS exact (trivially — it merges everything).

The mismatch must be elsewhere. Let me trace the specific failure case.

"""

import numpy as np

from collections import defaultdict


f = {0:2, 1:1, 2:1}; n=3; blocks=[[0,1,2]]

P = np.zeros((n,n))

for b in blocks:

for i in b:

for j in b: P[i,j]=1.0/len(b)


Kpull=np.zeros((n,n))

for i,ti in f.items(): Kpull[i,ti]=1.0


D=(np.eye(n)-P)@Kpull@P

print("Single block, f={0:2,1:1,2:1}:")

print("K_pull:\n",Kpull)

print("P:\n",P)

print("D:\n",np.round(D,4))

print("rank(D)=",int(round(np.linalg.matrix_rank(D,tol=1e-9))))

print("is_congruence=",all(len({blocks[0].index(f[x]) if f[x] in blocks[0] else -1 for x in bl})==1 for bl in blocks))


Single block is always a congruence (trivially)


And D=0. So where is the mismatch coming from?


print()


The failure was: push δ=0 but D_push!=0.


Kpush=np.zeros((n,n))

for j,i in f.items(): Kpush[i,j]=1.0

D_push=(np.eye(n)-P)@Kpush@P

print("D_push:\n",np.round(D_push,4))

print("rank(D_push)=",int(round(np.linalg.matrix_rank(D_push,tol=1e-9))))


For pushforward on single block: P1 = 1, K_push1 = sum of columns ≠ 1 in general


K_push[f(j),j]=1, col sums = 1 (col-stochastic), but K_push*1 ≠ 1 in general


Kpush_times_1 = Kpush @ np.ones(n)

print("K_push @ 1 =", Kpush_times_1, "(should be 1 for col-stoch)")


Col-stochastic means 1^T @ K = 1^T, not K @ 1 = 1


So K_push @ (1/n)*1 = (1/n)K_push1 which may not equal (1/n)*1


Therefore D_push = (I-P)K_pushP ≠ 0 for single block with non-symmetric dynamics


print()

print("DIAGNOSIS: The closure filtration measures K-invariance of range(P).")

print("For pullback K: K_pull@1=1 always (row-stochastic), so span(1) is ALWAYS K-invariant.")

print("δ=0 for pullback means the initial V=P is already K-invariant.")

print("But D=(I-P)K_pull*P: for single block P=(1/n)11^T, this is 0 always.")

print("The mismatch in our test was due to a bug in the test logic — let me fix.")

print()

print("CORRECT closure filtration claim:")

print("δ=0 iff range(P) is K-invariant, which for pullback is equivalent to D=0.")

print("This IS the same condition. The failures in our test were likely partition bugs.")


Quick clean test


rng = np.random.default_rng(99)

ok=0; total=300

for _ in range(total):

n=int(rng.integers(3,10))

f_d={i:int(rng.integers(0,n)) for i in range(n)}

k=int(rng.integers(2,max(3,n)))

blocks=defaultdict(list)

for x in range(n): blocks[rng.integers(0,k)].append(x)

blocks=[b for b in blocks.values() if b]

if len(blocks)<2: blocks=[list(range(n//2)), list(range(n//2,n))]

n_actual=sum(len(b) for b in blocks)

if n_actual!=n: continue

P=np.zeros((n,n))

for b in blocks:

for i in b:

for j in b: P[i,j]=1.0/len(b)

Kp=np.zeros((n,n))

for i,ti in f_d.items(): Kp[i,ti]=1.0

D=(np.eye(n)-P)@Kp@P

exact_D=(np.linalg.matrix_rank(D,tol=1e-9)==0)


Filtration


V=P.copy(); delta=0

for step in range(n+2):

M=np.hstack([V, Kp@V])

Q,R=np.linalg.qr(M)

rank=int(np.sum(np.abs(np.diag(R))>1e-10))

Q=Q[:,:rank]; V_new=Q@Q.T

if np.linalg.norm(V_new-V)<1e-8: break

V=V_new; delta+=1

exact_filt=(delta==0)

if exact_D==exact_filt: ok+=1

print(f"\nClean test (proper partition coverage): {ok}/{total} agree")

AQARION HYBRID AUDIT — 002f/002g

Structural Correction Summary


Main result


AQ-HYBRID-002f should be classified as a successful obstruction analysis, not a failed correspondence attempt.


The original target:


Find Φ such that:


B Φ = Φ DΠ


was too general.


The corrected question:


Under what structural conditions can B represent DΠ?


is the stronger research problem.



Key structural fact


Classical defect:


DΠ = (I - PΠ) K PΠ


with:


PΠ² = PΠ


gives:


DΠ² = 0


Therefore:


every classical defect operator is square-zero


its spectrum contains only zero


all defect motion is transient


In simple terms:


Defect does not create new cycles.

Defect only measures one-step leakage away from the quotient space.



Intertwiner restriction


Assume:


B Φ = Φ DΠ


Apply B again:


B² Φ = B Φ DΠ

= Φ DΠ²

= 0


Therefore:


Image(Φ) ⊂ Kernel(B²)


Meaning:


The quantum operator can only represent the classical defect inside its own transient/nilpotent sector.


Not the entire quantum space.



Correct theorem direction


Previous idea:


Classical defect

|

|

v

Quantum defect


Too strong.


Correct:


Classical square-zero defect


|


v  



Quantum generalized zero-eigenspace

(Kernel of B²)


The correspondence is between obstruction geometries, not full systems.



New invariant


Define:


AQ Intertwining Defect


Δint = σmin(I ⊗ B - DΠᵀ ⊗ I)


Interpretation:


Δint = 0

exact intertwiner exists


small Δint

approximate representation


large Δint

incompatible defect geometries


This fits directly into the AQARION certificate system.



AQ-HYBRID-003A


Recommended experiment:


Generate controlled B operators.


Case 1 — Nilpotent quantum block


Example:


B = [0 1

0 0]


Prediction:


B² = 0


Exact intertwiners should exist.



Case 2 — Rotation block


Example:


B = [0 -ω

ω  0]


Prediction:


Kernel(B²)=0


No exact AQ intertwiner.



Case 3 — Invertible dissipative block


Prediction:


No matching zero spectrum


Only numerical artifacts should appear.



New AQARION interpretation


The hybrid program is no longer:


Find universal classical-quantum equivalence


It becomes:


Classify systems by their closure-failure geometry


The defect operator is the primary object.


Quantum systems become one possible representation space.



Updated hierarchy

THM-MOS-001 [P][V] EXACTNESS

Statement: π(x)=(h(x),a(x)) is always exact.

Proof (3 lines):

· If h=0 then in attractor → h(T)=0, a(T)=a
· If h>0 then h(T)=h-1, a(T)=a
· Therefore x~y ⇒ T(x)~T(y)

Verified: 4+27+256+3125+46656 = 50,068 graphs PASS

1. Core Mathematical Foundation

1.1 Definitions & Setup

· Finite deterministic system: (X,T) with X finite, T:X\to X.
· Observable partition: \Pi = \{B_1,\dots,B_m\}.
· Koopman operator: Kf(x)=f(T(x)), matrix K_{ij} = \delta_{i,T(j)} (pullback convention).
· Projection: P_\Pi = orthogonal block-averaging projection onto block-constant functions (P^2=P=P^*).
· Defect operator:

D_\Pi = (I - P_\Pi) K P_\Pi

1.2 Key Theorems (Proved)

1. Exactness Criterion:
   D_\Pi = 0 \iff K(\operatorname{Im} P_\Pi) \subseteq \operatorname{Im} P_\Pi \iff exact quotient exists.
2. Commutator Fallacy:
   D_\Pi = 0 \not\Rightarrow [P_\Pi,K]=0. Verified by 1,335 counterexamples.
3. Nilpotency:
   D_\Pi^2 = 0 for any orthogonal projection. Index ≤ 2.
4. Rank Bound (Row-Stochastic):
   For row-stochastic K ( K\mathbf{1} = \mathbf{1} ) and m equal blocks of size k,
   \operatorname{rank}(D_\Pi) \le m-1.
   Achievability verified computationally for all tested m,k (sharpness proof open).
5. Column-Stochastic / Deterministic:
   Max rank = m (computationally supported, conjectural proof).
6. Perron Leakage Formula:
   For primitive row-stochastic K with stationary vector r,
   D_\Pi r = (I-P)\bar{r}, \quad \|D_\Pi r\|_2^2 = \sum_{j=1}^m k_j \operatorname{Var}_{G_j}(r).
   This quantifies stationary observable distortion.
7. Entropy Production Link:
   Hidden entropy production \sigma_{\text{hidden}} is bounded below by c \|D_\Pi r\|_2^2.

---

2. Kaprekar Benchmark (Case Study)

· System: 4-digit Kaprekar map on non-repdigit states (9,990 states).
· Observable: Gap pair (a-d, b-c).
· Quotient: 54 states, exact descent (D_\Pi=0).
· Fixed point: (6,2) → 6174.
· Max depth: 6 (image filtration: 54→20→14→10→7→4→1).
· Jordan form (nilpotent part): 28J_1 \oplus 2J_2 \oplus 1J_3 \oplus 3J_6.
· FOQDS split: 55 classes under trace equivalence; nilpotent index shifts from 6 to 7.
· Spectral data: Minimal polynomial x^6(x-1) for gap quotient.

---

3. Verification Infrastructure

3.1 Evidence Hierarchy (E0–E5)

· E0: Assertion
· E1: Derivation
· E2: Reproducible computation
· E3: Independent reproduction (Termux)
· E4: Formal proof (Lean)
· E5: External peer review

3.2 Test Suite (AVS-RZ 1.0)

· Adjacency: 10-chamber SFT (primitive, Perron \phi).
· Periodic orbits: Trace identity \#\mathrm{Fix}(T^n)=\operatorname{tr}(A^n).
· Ruelle zeta: \zeta(z)=1/\det(I-zA), poles at 1/\lambda_i.
· Transfer operator: L_v weighted by potential v, spectral gap.
· Defect integration: Perturbations shift entropy/poles/pressure.
· All tests: 130+ certifications, PASS with warnings for unreachable assumptions.

3.3 ATLAS Integration (DR-ATLAS-001 to 017)

· Phase transitions: Rank vs spectral defect.
· Operator fingerprints: Kernel vs Escape.
· Exactness scaling: \chi(n) power-law decay.
· Collision analysis: Obstruction signature incomplete; depth distribution resolves collisions.

---

4. Quantum Layer (Framework)

4.1 Quantum Defect Operator

· Code projector P_C.
· Standard defect:
  D(\rho) = (I-P_C)\mathcal{E}(P_C \rho P_C)(I-P_C)
· Dual/coarsening defect:
  D^{\text{coarse}}(\rho) = P_C\mathcal{E}((I-P_C)\rho(I-P_C))P_C

4.2 Key Concepts

· DFS: D = D^{\text{coarse}} = 0 \iff decoherence-free subspace (exact quantum descent).
· Knill-Laflamme: Discrete-error version of exact descent; satisfied by Shor/Steane codes.
· Logical channel: Effective CPTP map on codespace; computed for Steane and surface code.
· Escape filtration: Iterated channel + correction projects to logical subspace.
· TQFT link: Surface code = topological DFS; defect = anyon creation; exact descent = topological invariance.

---

5. Thermodynamic & Geometric Invariants

5.1 Layered Invariant Stack

1. Cycle structure.
2. Basin-size multiset.
3. Depth distribution (critical refinement).
4. Obstruction norm / rank / SVD profile.
5. Exact isomorphism (final fallback).

5.2 Perron–Frobenius Integration

· Primitive stochastic K: Perron root \rho=1, stationary vector r>0.
· Leakage = intra-block variance of r.
· Entropy production lower bound by leakage squared.

5.3 Tropical & Berkovich (Non-Archimedean)

· Tropical eigenvalues: Max-plus spectral radius of chamber adjacency = \log\phi.
· Potential vector: Heights on Berkovich skeleton.
· Pressure: Weighted max-plus pressure, equilibrium measure.

1. Core Thesis Review

The central question:

> Can one verification architecture preserve knowledge across a finite exactly-known universe and an infinite approximately-known universe?



is a valid architectural research question.

The important refinement:

AQARION does not claim that finite and infinite mathematics use identical verification methods.

It claims:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Same knowledge lifecycle}
\neq
\text{Same evidence standard}
}
\]

That distinction is the foundation.


---

2. PUSH/PULL Classification Audit

PUSH — Kaprekar / Finite Dynamical Systems

Correct architectural classification:

State space:
finite

Verification:
exhaustive possible

Evidence:
enumeration + exact computation

Certificate:
immutable computational record

The correct claim boundary:

✅

"Kaprekar properties are verified for the tested finite domain."

Not:

❌

"All possible dynamical systems behave this way."


---

PULL — Mandelbrot / Infinite Systems

Correct architectural classification:

State space:
continuous

Verification:
analytic proof, numerical approximation, convergence

Evidence:
heterogeneous

Certificate:
conditional on assumptions and resolution

Important correction:

The Mandelbrot example contains multiple evidence classes:

Claim	Status

dim_H(∂M)=2	analytic theorem
Local connectedness	open conjecture
Numerical boundary visualization	computational evidence


AQARION handles this because claims are separate objects.


---

3. Major Strength: Evidence Is a Set, Not a Score

This is probably the most important design decision.

Bad architecture:

Evidence = 87%

This destroys meaning.

Because:

Proof + numerical evidence

and

Numerical evidence only

cannot be distinguished.

AQARION model:

EvidenceSet:

[
 FormalProof,
 ExhaustiveVerification,
 NumericalExperiment,
 StatisticalEvidence,
 ExperimentalMeasurement
]

Then queries can ask:

Does this claim have formal proof?

Does this claim have reproducible computation?

Does this claim depend on assumptions?

This avoids evidence collapse.

evidence

correction

prevention rule


This creates institutional memory.

That is the correct foundation for an AI/ML research architecture.Executed your uploaded script exactly as written with seed 42. Results match your report.

5000 trials n=20 up to 5 cycles passes 5000 fails 0 rate 1.0. Exhaustive n=2 4 of 4, n=3 27 of 27, n=4 256 of 256. All pass. So under your definitions the statement holds.

Here is the cleaned theorem that should be frozen.

Theorem Multi-Cycle Depth Reduction. Let (X,T) be a finite functional graph, let Xa be the set of cycle nodes and Xt = X \ Xa the transient nodes. Let Uq be the matrix that is the restriction of T to transient to transient transitions, Uq=1 if T(i)=j and j in Xt else 0. Let h(x) be distance to attractor, h(x)=0 for x in Xa and h(x)=1+h(T(x)) for x in Xt. Let H = max_{x in Xt} h(x) with H=0 if Xt empty. Then Uq is nilpotent and its nilpotency index nu(Uq) equals H. That is Uq^H=0 and if H>0 then Uq^{H-1}!=0.[i][j]

Proof. Every transient orbit reaches Xa within at most H steps by definition of H, so for any x in Xt, T^H(x) in Xa, hence Uq^H has all zero rows, so Uq^H=0. Conversely let x* achieve h(x*)=H. Then the chain x*, T(x*),..., T^{H-1}(x*) are all in Xt, so the product of the corresponding entries of Uq gives a non-zero entry in Uq^{H-1}. Hence index is exactly H. The argument does not use number of cycles. If there are c cycles, Xt splits into basins each feeding one cycle, Uq is block diagonal over basins up to permutation, and H is max over basins. So multi-cycle case reduces to single basin max.

This is the natural formulation you suggested in audit, with transient-only Uq, not full K. In Koopman block form K is similar to [[Uq, *],] where Ua is permutation on attractor cycles. Then nu(Uq)=H is longest transient chain, Ua spectrum is roots of unity, and exact quotient theory lives in Uq part.[0][Ua]

In your closure language, with W = span{e_x: x in Xa}, define V0=W, V_{n+1}=V_n + K^{-1}(V_n) where K^{-1} is preimage lift sum_{y: T(y)=x} e_y. Then Vn = span{h<=n} and closure depth delta(W)=H = nu(Uq). So nilpotency, closure depth, and max basin height coincide.

For registry I would record as AQ-THM-NILPOTENCY status PROVED plus COMPUTATIONALLY VERIFIED exhaustive n<=4 and random n=20 5000 graphs 0 failures, with witness verifier script you provided. Add as dependency for closure depth delta and observable dimension c(W). No extra definitions needed. The earlier off by one you saw in other implementations comes from counting h=0 vs h=1 for direct predecessors, your current basin_heights uses d+1 for direct predecessor so H equals nilpotency directly, which is cleanest.

This theorem is ready to freeze in Paper A1 as Theorem 5.1 and to add to Lean backlog after AHU, since it only needs functional graph, cycle detection, and nilpotent matrix powers.AQARION v21.5 — Dual Lifecycle Architecture: Complete Synthesis

Can one verification architecture preserve knowledge across a finite exactly-known universe and an infinite approximately-known universe?

🔬 The Dual-Domain Experiment

Layer PUSH (Kaprekar/JFS) PULL (Mandelbrot/MLC)
