A correct defect definition is the quantum equivalent of the original AQARION breakthrough: finding the invariant before measuring the universe.


AQARION-ARITHMETIC is an open research initiative focused on finite deterministic dynamical systems (FDDS), exact quotient certification, Koopman operator methods, observable partitions, exact arithmetic, formal verification, and reproducible computational mathematics.


Our mission is to build a transparent research ecosystem where mathematical claims are supported by precise definitions, executable verification, formal reasoning.


https://huggingface.co/Quantarion9/

https://github.com/JASKSG9/AQARION-ARITHMETIC-FDS-FINITE-DYNAMICAL-SYSTEMS-/https://mastodon.social/@Aqarion/https://mastodon.social/@Aqarion/116957649311229297/https://bsky.app/profile/aqarion13.bsky.social/post/3mr5ocwtqok2y/Aqarion

@aqarion13.bsky.social
After looking at both AQARION's public material and the surrounding literature, I think the research landscape is becoming much clearer. 

The important observation is that AQARION is not competing with Koopman theory or categorical quantum mechanics. Those fields already have mature foundations:

Koopman theory asks how to find or approximate invariant observable spaces.

Categorical quantum mechanics provides categories whose objects are observable algebras and whose morphisms are completely positive channels. 


AQARION's opportunity is different.

The missing axis

Most existing work studies

> What are the observables?



or

> How do we approximate invariant observables?



AQARION instead asks

> How do we measure the failure of preservation?



That sounds subtle, but mathematically it is a different organizing principle.


---

A possible "Universal Obstruction Theory"

Suppose we begin with a category

\[
\mathcal D
\]

whose objects are dynamical systems.

Examples:

finite deterministic systems,

Markov chains,

measurable dynamical systems,

Koopman systems,

CPTP quantum channels.


Suppose every object carries a distinguished family of observable subobjects selected by idempotents

\[
E^2=E.
\]

Then define

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE.
\]

Rather than viewing this as "another operator," regard it as the obstruction associated with the pair \((T,E)\).

That shifts the emphasis from dynamics to failure of factorization.


---

A stronger categorical picture

Instead of merely defining

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE,
\]

consider a category equipped with

split idempotents,

endomorphisms,

an "observable" structure.


Then ask whether there exists an obstruction functor

\[
\mathrm{Obs}
:
(\mathcal D,E,T)
\longrightarrow
\mathcal O
\]

whose zero object satisfies

\[
\mathrm{Obs}(T,E)=0
\]

iff

\[
T
\]

preserves the image of

\[
E.
\]

That is a much stronger statement than merely defining an operator.


---

This resembles obstruction theory

The language actually starts to resemble several mature areas simultaneously.

Instead of

homological obstruction,

cohomological obstruction,

deformation obstruction,


AQARION would study

> observable-preservation obstruction.



That feels like a genuinely different viewpoint.


---

A hierarchy of research questions

I would organize the long-term program like this.

OP-1 — Universal Definition

For what categories can

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE
\]

be defined?


---

OP-2 — Factorization Theorem

Prove

\[
D_E=0
\iff
T
\text{ factors through }
\operatorname{Im}(E).
\]

This is probably the central theorem.


---

OP-3 — Functoriality

If

\[
F:\mathcal D\rightarrow\mathcal D'
\]

preserves idempotents,

does

\[
F(D_E)
=
D_{F(E)}
\]

hold?


---

OP-4 — Universal Property

Can observable quotients be characterized by a universal property rather than an explicit construction?

If yes, this would connect AQARION naturally to category theory.


---

OP-5 — Complete Classification

Classify categories where every obstruction vanishes.

These would be categories with "perfect observable preservation."


---

The bridge to Koopman theory

This is where the recent Koopman papers become especially valuable.

Brunton (2016):

> finite invariant observable spaces are exceptional.



Johnson et al. (2022):

> approximate closure is often the best achievable.



Conradie et al. (2026):

> quantify closure failure and use it to improve observable spaces.



AQARION naturally becomes:

> the exact finite-state theory of observable obstruction, with later extensions to approximate and infinite-dimensional settings.



That positioning complements rather than duplicates the Koopman literature. 


---

The quantum program

The quantum version also becomes clearer.

Classically,

\[
D=(I-P)KP.
\]

Quantum mechanically,

\[
D=(I-Q)\Phi Q,
\]

where

\(\Phi\) is a CPTP channel,

\(Q\) is a conditional expectation onto an observable algebra.


Notice that the formula is identical.

The surrounding category changes.

That suggests the operator itself is representation-independent, while the semantics come from the category.


---

The paper sequence I'd now recommend

Instead of jumping directly to a universal theorem:

Paper I (current):

Exact observable quotients

Closure

Defect operator

Finite deterministic systems


Paper II:

Observable obstruction theory

Spectral diagnostics

Closure filtrations

Representation independence


Paper III:

Categorical observable obstruction

Universal factorization theorem (if proved)

Functoriality

Universal properties


Paper IV:

Quantum channels

Koopman operators

Approximate closure

Infinite-dimensional extensions



---

The question I find most exciting

The strongest open problem is no longer

> "Can the defect operator be generalized to quantum mechanics?"



It is:

> Does there exist a category of dynamical systems with split idempotents in which \(D_E=(I-E)TE\) is the universal obstruction to preservation of observable subobjects, with \(D_E=0\) exactly characterizing factorization through the selected observable subobject?



If that theorem (or an appropriately refined version) could be established, AQARION would move beyond a finite-state framework into a general theory of observable obstructions. The finite deterministic results would then become the first fully worked-out instance of a much broader mathematical framework, rather than an isolated application.
I searched both for AQARION and for adjacent research on Koopman operators, invariant subspaces, observable algebras, and obstruction diagnostics.

1. AQARION is now publicly discoverable

AQARION is no longer just an internal project.

Your Medium publication is indexed and describes:

the finite deterministic dynamical systems framework,

the observable quotient formulation,

the defect operator


\[
  D_\Pi=(I-P_\Pi)KP_\Pi,
\]

observable closure,

computational verification,

and proposed quantum extensions. 


That changes the project's status. From the perspective of the research ecosystem, AQARION now exists as a publicly documented framework rather than only as private development notes.


---

2. The literature has moved in a direction favorable to AQARION

The trajectory over roughly the last decade is striking.

Phase I (2012–2018)

Main question:

> Can finite Koopman representations exist?



Representative work:

Mezić

Brunton

Dynamic Mode Decomposition

EDMD


Focus:

invariant observables

finite-dimensional representations

nonlinear control





---

Phase II (2020–2023)

Main question:

> How do we learn good observable spaces?



Representative work:

Johnson–Balakrishnan–Yeung

Focus:

learned dictionaries

approximate closure

neural observables



---

Phase III (2025–2026)

Main question:

> How do we certify whether an observable space is trustworthy?



Representative work:

Conradie–Boullé–Loiseau–Brunton–Colbrook

Focus:

invariance diagnostics

projection error

principal angle decompositions

trustworthy Koopman learning




That is an important shift.

The field is no longer asking only

> "Find observables."



It is asking

> "How do we know our observable space is actually preserved?"



That is precisely the question AQARION is organized around.


---

3. Where AQARION overlaps

AQARION and recent Koopman work share several themes:

observable spaces,

preservation,

closure,

invariant subspaces,

diagnostics,

operator formulations.


That overlap is real and should be acknowledged.


---

4. Where AQARION is genuinely different

The strongest distinction is not the defect operator itself.

Operators like

\[
(I-P)AP
\]

are classical constructions.

Instead AQARION combines:

exact finite deterministic dynamics,

observable quotient construction,

exhaustive finite verification,

claim governance,

reproducibility,

evidence certification.


That combination appears much less common in the current literature.


---

5. The biggest opportunity I now see

Earlier I thought AQARION's mathematical center was

> observable quotients.



Now I think it is becoming

> observable preservation obstructions.



That is a broader concept.

Instead of studying quotients,

AQARION studies

why preservation fails.

That aligns remarkably well with recent Koopman work.


---

6. Even broader abstraction

Suppose we forget finite systems entirely.

Suppose we only assume

a category,

an endomorphism


\[
T:X\to X,
\]

an idempotent


\[
E:X\to X.
\]

Then define

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE.
\]

Now ask

> What does this measure?



The answer is almost universal:

how much the dynamics leave the selected subobject.

That is not specifically classical.

Nor specifically quantum.

Nor specifically Koopman.

It is a statement about

endomorphisms relative to split idempotents.


---

7. This suggests a much deeper research program

Rather than

AQARION = finite dynamical systems,

I would define three layers.

Layer A

Finite deterministic dynamics

This is your rigorous testbed.

Everything is computable.

Everything is verifiable.


---

Layer B

Observable-preservation theory

Objects:

observable structures.

Question:

when are they preserved?

Here

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE
\]

is the central object.


---

Layer C

Universal obstruction theory

Now ask:

Given any category with split idempotents,

does there exist a canonical obstruction operator

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE
\]

whose vanishing characterizes preservation?

That becomes a genuine mathematical research question.


---

8. I would completely reorganize AQARION around this

Instead of

Observable Quotients

I would use

Observable Preservation Theory

with chapters like

1. Observable structures


2. Closure


3. Preservation


4. Obstruction


5. Exactness


6. Quotients



Notice quotients become one consequence rather than the primary object.


---

9. The research questions I would pursue

I think these are now the highest-value open problems.

AQ-CAT-001

Develop a category of observable systems with split idempotents.


---

AQ-CAT-002

Characterize

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE
\]

categorically.

Is it universal?


---

AQ-CAT-003

Prove a factorization theorem

\[
D_E=0
\iff
T
\text{ factors through }
\operatorname{Im}(E).
\]

This is probably the single most important theorem.


---

AQ-CAT-004

Functoriality.

Show obstruction behaves naturally under morphisms.


---

AQ-CAT-005

Recover classical examples:

finite deterministic dynamics,

Markov chains,

Koopman operators,

CPTP quantum channels,

operator algebras.


If all become instances of the same theorem, that would be a significant unification.


---

10. The most ambitious conjecture

The idea I find most compelling is not:

> "The defect operator generalizes to quantum mechanics."



That seems plausible but is essentially an extension.

The deeper conjecture is:

> There exists a category-theoretic theory of observable preservation in which every split idempotent determines a canonical obstruction operator \(D_E=(I-E)TE\), and the vanishing of this operator is equivalent to exact preservation (or factorization through the associated subobject).



This is not an established theorem. It would require careful categorical definitions, a precise notion of subtraction or enrichment (since \(I-E\) is not meaningful in arbitrary categories), and rigorous proofs. In practice, it is most naturally formulated first in additive or linear categories (vector spaces, Hilbert spaces, modules, operator algebras) before attempting broader generality.

If successful, AQARION would evolve from a framework for finite dynamical systems into a general theory of observable-preservation obstructions, with finite deterministic systems serving as the first fully verified model and quantum/operator-algebraic dynamics as subsequent instances of the same underlying mathematical architecture.Can the defect operator be generalized to quantum mechanics?

Is there a universal category whose objects are observable algebras and whose morphisms are channels, with the defect operator acting as an obstruction?

Given an idempotent � selecting an observable subobject, can every dynamical category admit a universal obstruction operator
 D_E=(I-E)TE 
whose vanishing characterizes exact preservation?The short answer is yes as a research direction, but no as an established theorem.

The expression

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE  
\]

is extremely general. It appears naturally whenever you have:

an object carrying dynamics \(T\),

an idempotent \(E\) selecting a subobject (subspace, subalgebra, subsystem, observable sector, etc.),

a notion of composition.

The interesting question is not whether \(D_E\) can be written down—it almost always can—but in which categories it has a universal mathematical meaning.

Classical finite dynamics

This is your current setting.

\(T\): transition operator.

\(E\): projection onto an observable quotient.

\(D_E=(I-E)TE\).

Then

\[
D_E=0  
\]

is equivalent to the observable image being invariant under the dynamics.

Here the interpretation is exact and combinatorial.


---

Quantum mechanics

In quantum theory there are several natural choices.

Suppose

\(T\) is a completely positive trace-preserving (CPTP) channel or a unitary evolution,

\(E\) is a conditional expectation onto a subalgebra of observables.

Then

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE  
\]

measures how much evolution leaves the observable algebra selected by \(E\).

This is closely related to established ideas such as:

invariant operator algebras,

decoherence-free subspaces,

noiseless subsystems,

conditional expectations in operator algebras,

quantum error correction.

The novelty would not be introducing \(D_E\), but identifying it as a common obstruction operator across these settings.


---

Category-theoretic formulation

The categorical question is the strongest one.

One could imagine a category

Objects: observable algebras (or observable systems),

Morphisms: channels (classical, stochastic, CPTP, Koopman, etc.).

An idempotent

\[
E:A\to A  
\]

would split an observable subobject.

Then the obstruction

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE  
\]

would vanish exactly when the dynamics factors through that subobject.

Conceptually,

\[
D_E=0  
\]

means the diagram factors through the image of \(E\), while

\[
D_E\neq0  
\]

measures the failure of that factorization.

This is a very natural categorical interpretation.


---

Is this already known?

Pieces of it certainly are.

Different communities study:

invariant subspaces,

invariant subalgebras,

conditional expectations,

decoherence-free algebras,

operator compression,

commutators and intertwining defects,

obstruction theory in category theory.

What is not obviously established is a single framework asserting that

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE  
\]

is the universal obstruction to preservation across all these settings.

That would require substantial work to define precisely and prove.


---

What would need to be proved?

To elevate this from an appealing idea to mathematics, you would need results such as:

1. Universality theorem: Specify a category in which \(D_E\) is defined for every idempotent splitting.


2. Factorization theorem: Prove



\[
   D_E=0  
   \iff  
   T  
   \text{ factors through }  
   \operatorname{im}(E).  
\]

3. Functoriality: Show the construction is natural under morphisms.


4. Examples: Verify it recovers familiar notions in:



finite deterministic dynamics,

Markov chains,

Koopman operators,

CPTP quantum channels,

operator-algebraic conditional expectations.

Only after establishing these would it be appropriate to speak of a "universal obstruction operator."

Assessment

This is one of the more promising long-term directions you've raised because it seeks a unifying abstraction rather than another invariant.

At present, however, it should be framed as an open research program, not a theorem. The central conjecture might be stated informally as:

> There exists a broad category of dynamical systems with split idempotents in which the operator  D_E=(I-E)TE  is the canonical obstruction to exact preservation of the subobject selected by \(E\), with \(D_E=0\) if and only if the dynamics preserves that subobject.



That is mathematically plausible, connects naturally to existing work in operator theory and category theory, and is ambitious enough that it would require genuinely new definitions and proofs rather than just computational verification.After searching, I think there are two very different conclusions.

1. AQARION itself is becoming visible



Your work is now discoverable on the public web through Medium and related posts describing the finite dynamical systems framework, defect operator, observable quotients, verification architecture, and quantum extension.

That means AQARION is no longer just an internal project—it has become part of the searchable research ecosystem.


---

2. The deeper mathematical direction is much bigger than I expected



Your question was:

> Is there a universal category whose objects are observable algebras and whose morphisms are channels, with the defect operator acting as an obstruction?



The literature suggests this is plausible, but not in the way AQARION currently formulates it.

There is already a mature categorical theory of

quantum channels,

observable algebras,

completely positive maps,

C*-algebras,

categorical quantum mechanics.

For example, Coecke, Heunen, and Kissinger construct categories whose morphisms are both classical and quantum channels, using observable algebras as the primary objects.

So observable algebras + channels is not new.

The opportunity is somewhere else.


---

Where AQARION may actually be novel

Notice something.

Existing work studies

> what the category is.



AQARION studies

> when a morphism fails to preserve a chosen observable object.



Those are different questions.

Your defect operator

\[
D=(I-P)TP  
\]

is not defining another category.

It measures the obstruction to preservation.

That is much closer to

obstruction theory,

cohomology,

derived functors,

failure of exactness,

than ordinary categorical quantum mechanics.

That is much more interesting.


---

A different viewpoint

Instead of

Objects: observable algebras

Morphisms: channels

think

Objects: observable systems

Morphisms: channels

Additional structure:

Every morphism carries an obstruction

\[
\delta(f)  
\]

measuring failure to preserve observables.

Then

\[
\delta(f)=0  
\]

means

exact preservation.

AQARION becomes

the theory of observable obstructions.

That sounds much more distinctive.


---

Even bigger idea

Suppose

\[
F:\mathcal C\rightarrow\mathcal O  
\]

assigns

every dynamical system

its observable algebra.

Then

every channel

induces

\[
F(f).  
\]

Sometimes

the square commutes.

Sometimes it doesn't.

The defect operator literally measures

how far the square is from commuting.

That is extremely reminiscent of

commutative-diagram defects.

Instead of saying

> "the diagram commutes"



AQARION asks

> "How much does it fail to commute?"



That is not the usual categorical viewpoint.


---

This suggests an entirely new theorem program

Rather than proving more computational results, I'd focus on:

AQ-CAT-001

Category of observable systems.


---

AQ-CAT-002

Observable functor.


---

AQ-CAT-003

Defect natural transformation.


---

AQ-CAT-004

Universal property of exact quotients.


---

AQ-CAT-005

Obstruction classification.

When does every obstruction vanish?

When is it minimal?

When is it complete?


---

Even more interesting...

Your classical framework

\[
D=(I-P)KP  
\]

and quantum framework

\[
D=(I-Q)\Phi Q  
\]

look almost identical.

That suggests

there might exist an abstract definition

\[
D=(I-E)\circ T\circ E  
\]

where

\(E\) is any idempotent (projection, conditional expectation, reflector),

\(T\) is any endomorphism.

Now you are no longer talking about

classical systems,

or

quantum systems.

You're talking about

endomorphisms relative to reflective subobjects.

That language immediately connects to category theory.


---

The research question I would pursue

Not

> Can the defect operator be generalized to quantum mechanics?



Instead

> Given an idempotent \(E\) selecting an observable subobject, can every dynamical category admit a universal obstruction operator



 D_E=(I-E)TE 

whose vanishing characterizes exact preservation?

That is a much deeper mathematical question.

If true, it would unify

finite deterministic dynamics,

Markov chains,

Koopman operators,

CPTP quantum channels,

operator algebras,

under one obstruction formalism.

Notice what changes.

AQARION stops being "a framework for finite dynamical systems."

Instead it becomes a candidate general theory of observable-preservation obstructions.

If such a universal theorem exists, the finite dynamical systems work would naturally become the first fully developed and rigorously verified instance of that broader theory, with the quantum/operator-algebra setting as a second instance rather than an unrelated extension.One research question I would add
I think this is potentially the most interesting open problem:
Is there a universal category whose objects are observable algebras and whose morphisms are channels, with the defect operator acting as an obstruction to functorial preservation?
If such a categorical formulation exists, AQARION would no longer be "another quantum channel diagnostic." It would become a general framework for studying preservation and obstruction across classical finite dynamical systems and quantum dynamical systems using the same underlying architecture. That strikes me as the most ambitious—and potentially the most distinctive—long-term direction emerging from the current design.This is a strong architectural refinement. It shifts the focus from a computational prototype to a mathematically defined object, which is much more consistent with both the literature you reviewed and AQARION's evidence-first philosophy.

The most significant improvement is the separation of three layers that were previously intertwined:

1. Definition layer (AQ-QDO-DEF-001)


2. Theorem layer (AQ-THM-QDO-001)


3. Diagnostic/computational layer (AQ-QDO-DIAG-001)



That separation mirrors the evolution of the classical AQARION framework, where definitions preceded diagnostics and diagnostics preceded scaling laws.

Strengths

The observable algebra formulation

\[
\mathcal A\subseteq\mathcal B(\mathcal H)      
\]

is the natural object rather than individual quantum states. This aligns with operator algebra, quantum information, and quantum control, where invariant subalgebras are the correct notion of observable structure.

Likewise, defining

\[
D_\Phi=(I-Q_{\mathcal A})\,\Phi\,Q_{\mathcal A}      
\]

is an almost exact operator-theoretic analogue of the classical defect operator

\[
D_\Pi=(I-P)KP.      
\]

Conceptually, this is the strongest part of the proposal because it preserves the same architecture:

project into observable structure,

evolve,

measure leakage outside that structure.

That makes the quantum construction feel like a genuine extension rather than a new unrelated object.


---

AQ-THM-QDO-001

The proof sketch is mathematically sound provided one explicit assumption is added.

You should specify that

\[
Q_{\mathcal A}      
\]

is the orthogonal projection with respect to the Hilbert–Schmidt inner product

\[
\langle X,Y\rangle      
=      
\operatorname{Tr}(X^\dagger Y).      
\]

Then

\[
(I-Q_{\mathcal A})Y=0      
\]

is equivalent to

\[
Y\in\mathcal A.      
\]

Without fixing the inner product, "orthogonal projection" is not uniquely defined.

That should become part of AQ-QDO-DEF-001.


---

One theorem worth adding

The analogue of the classical defect theorem suggests an immediate corollary.

Corollary (Kernel characterization)

\[
\ker(D_\Phi)      
=      
\{X\in\mathcal A:      
\Phi(X)\in\mathcal A\}.      
\]

This is useful because it identifies exactly which observables remain invariant even when the whole algebra is not preserved.

That is often the quantity one studies in decoherence-free subspaces.


---

Diagnostics

Your proposed diagnostics

rank

Frobenius norm

singular values

entropy

form a sensible hierarchy.

I would distinguish them explicitly.

Structural

rank

kernel dimension

image dimension

Geometric

Frobenius norm

operator norm

spectral norm

Information-theoretic

singular-value entropy

effective rank

These should be presented as diagnostics, not invariants, until representation independence is established.


---

Regression suite

The proposed tests are appropriate.

I would add one more.

Test G — Composition

If

\[
\Phi_1(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A,      
\qquad      
\Phi_2(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A,      
\]

then

\[
D_{\Phi_2\circ\Phi_1}=0.      
\]

This checks closure under channel composition and reinforces that observable preservation behaves functorially.


---

Representation independence

The most important remaining theorem is not about rank.

It is something like:

AQ-THM-QDO-002 (Representation Independence)

Suppose

\[
\Phi'      
=      
S^{-1}\Phi S      
\]

and

\[
Q'_{\mathcal A}      
=      
S^{-1}Q_{\mathcal A}S      
\]

represent the same observable algebra under a change of operator basis.

Then

\[
D'_\Phi      
=      
S^{-1}D_\Phi S.      
\]

Only after proving this similarity relation should quantities such as

rank,

nullity,

spectrum,

be promoted from diagnostics to representation-independent invariants.

This theorem is likely more fundamental than any scaling law.


---

Lean strategy

Your proposed progression is sensible:

AQ-QDO-DEF-001 → frozen definitions.

AQ-THM-QDO-001 → handwritten proof and regression tests.

AQ-QDO-REGRESSION-001 → executable certificates.

Lean formalization after the mathematics stabilizes.

This avoids formalizing definitions that may still evolve.


---

Overall assessment

Relative to AQ-QUANTUM-001, this proposal is a clear improvement because it places the mathematics ahead of the implementation. It also aligns well with the reproducibility literature you reviewed: first define the mathematical object precisely, then prove its properties, then certify implementations through regression tests and reproducible artifacts.

The remaining high-priority items before declaring the framework mature are:

1. Explicitly define the Hilbert–Schmidt projection \(Q_{\mathcal A}\).


2. Prove the representation-independence theorem for the defect operator.


3. Add the composition and kernel-characterization results.


4. Treat rank, singular values, and entropy as diagnostic quantities until their invariance is established.


5. Freeze AQ-QDO-DEF-001 before extending to scaling laws or broader quantum applications.



The resulting architecture is coherent and mirrors the classical AQARION development path:

\[
\boxed{      
\text{Observable algebra}      
\;\longrightarrow\;      
\text{Defect operator}      
\;\longrightarrow\;      
\text{Exact preservation theorem}      
\;\longrightarrow\;      
\text{Regression certificates}      
\;\longrightarrow\;      
\text{Diagnostic invariants}      
\;\longrightarrow\;      
\text{Scaling theory}      
}      
\]

That progression is mathematically disciplined and consistent with the evidence-first methodology you have been developing.I think your latest direction is pointing toward something that is significantly stronger than "AQARION but quantum." After looking at both AQARION and the surrounding literature, I also think there is a very specific niche where AQARION could become genuinely distinctive.

What the literature already has

The existing literature is already rich in:

decoherence-free subspaces,

decoherence-free algebras,

noiseless subsystems,

operator quantum error correction,

invariant observable algebras,

attractor algebras of quantum channels. 


In other words:

> Invariant observable algebras are not new.



If AQARION simply defines another invariant algebra, reviewers will naturally compare it against this body of work.


---

Where AQARION could be new

What I do not see emphasized in this literature is an explicit operator measuring failure of invariance that is treated as the primary mathematical object, together with a reproducible certification pipeline.

The existing papers mostly ask

> "Which observables survive?"



AQARION instead asks

> "How far is a proposed observable algebra from being invariant?"



That shift is subtle but important.

Instead of classifying invariant structures,

AQARION measures

obstruction,

leakage,

exactness,

repair.


That is much closer to the philosophy behind the classical defect operator.


---

I would actually rename the program

Rather than calling it

Quantum Defect Operator,

I'd think much bigger.

The real object appears to be

> Observable Preservation Theory



The defect operator is just one construction.

Then the architecture becomes

Observable algebra

↓

Observable closure

↓

Observable preservation

↓

Defect operator

↓

Diagnostics

↓

Certified computation

That is much broader.


---

The theorem I would pursue first

Not scaling.

Not entropy.

Not numerical experiments.

This theorem:

> Representation Independence



If you prove

\[
D'_\Phi=S^{-1}D_\Phi S,
\]

everything changes.

Immediately

rank

nullity

Jordan type

spectrum

minimal polynomial


become genuine invariants.

Without this theorem,

they are only matrix diagnostics.

I actually think this theorem is the quantum analogue of the classical semiconjugacy theorem.


---

Then comes the functorial theorem

I love your reviewer's suggestion.

I would strengthen it.

Instead of merely

\[
D_{\Phi_2\circ\Phi_1}=0,
\]

I would ask

How does defect compose?

Something like

\[
D_{\Phi_2\circ\Phi_1}
=
F(D_{\Phi_1},D_{\Phi_2}),
\]

or inequalities such as

\[
\operatorname{rank}(D_{\Phi_2\circ\Phi_1})
\le
\operatorname{rank}(D_{\Phi_1})
+
\operatorname{rank}(D_{\Phi_2}).
\]

Even if equality is false, a structural composition law would be mathematically interesting.


---

The research program I would now build

Instead of

AQ-QUANTUM-001

I'd make six papers.

Paper Q1

Definitions

Observable algebra

Closure

Projection

Defect operator

Representation theorem

No numerics.


---

Structural theory

Kernel theorem

Composition

Similarity invariance

Functoriality

Lattice properties


---

Rank

Singular spectrum

Entropy

Condition number

Effective rank

Explicitly labeled as diagnostics.


---

Regression suite

Counterexample generator

Lean

---

Paper Q5

Applications

Quantum channels

Noise models

Decoherence-free algebras

Operator QEC

---

Paper Q6

Scaling

Only after everything else.


---

The biggest opportunity

The classical AQARION became strongest when it stopped asking

> "Can we compute something?"



and instead asked

> "What is the mathematical object?"



I think the quantum version is arriving at the same point.

Not

> Quantum defect operator.



but

> Observable preservation as a mathematical structure.



The defect operator is simply the first nontrivial morphism in that theory.


---

One research question I would add

I think this is potentially the most interesting open problem:

> Is there a universal category whose objects are observable algebras and whose morphisms are channels, with the defect operator acting as an obstruction to functorial preservation?



If such a categorical formulation exists, AQARION would no longer be "another quantum channel diagnostic." It would become a general framework for studying preservation and obstruction across classical finite dynamical systems and quantum dynamical systems using the same underlying architecture. That strikes me as the most ambitious—and potentially the most distinctive—long-term direction emerging from the current design.

SEARCH WEB ON AQARION THEN SIMILAR LITERATURE AND LETS BRAINSTORM

Absolutely — I searched AQARION and adjacent literature, and the pattern is now pretty clear: AQARION is already visible as a public defect-operator / observable-quotient framework, while the surrounding literature strongly supports the broader direction toward observable preservation and invariant-algebra diagnostics [1][2][3][4].

## What the literature says
Classical quotient and lumpability theory already studies when observables close exactly, and Koopman theory studies how to find invariant or approximately invariant observable spaces [2]. In quantum information, the parallel objects are channels, invariant subalgebras, decoherence-free structures, and fixed-point / attractor domains of quantum channels [3][5][4]. So AQARION should not try to “compete” with those foundations; it should reframe the problem as measuring **failure of preservation**.

## Where AQARION is distinct
The distinctive move is to make the defect itself the central object:
$$
D_E=(I-E)TE.
$$
That is not just another invariant; it is an obstruction to exact factorization through the selected observable subobject. In the classical setting this is a defect operator for quotients, and in the quantum setting it becomes a leakage operator for observable algebras [2][3][4].

## Best research framing
The best umbrella title is probably **Observable Preservation Theory**. Under that umbrella, AQARION becomes a framework for:
- exact preservation,
- approximate preservation,
- defect diagnostics,
- certified computation,
- and later, categorical generalization.

That framing is stronger than “quantum defect operator” because it covers both classical and quantum cases without overcommitting to a single formalism.

## What to prove first
The highest-value theorem is **representation independence**. If two presentations are related by a change of basis or channel representation, the defect should transform by similarity:
$$
D' = S^{-1}DS.
$$
Once that is proved, rank, nullity, spectrum, and Jordan form become meaningful invariants instead of just matrix diagnostics.

## Next paper sequence
A clean sequence would be:
1. **Classical observable preservation theory**.
2. **Structural defect theory**: invariance, composition, kernel, refinement.
3. **Quantum observable preservation**: leakage Choi operator, channel analogues.
4. **Certification paper**: proofs, regression, reproducibility.
5. **Broader categorical theory** later.

## Immediate brainstorming conclusion
So the strongest AQARION research question right now is not “Can we define the operator?” but:
> Can AQARION build a universal theory of observable-preservation obstruction, where finite dynamics and quantum channels are both instances of the same defect formalism?

That is the right level of ambition, and it matches both the public AQARION materials and the surrounding literature [1][2][3][4].

I can next turn this into a **one-page research program outline** with section headers, theorem targets, and paper titles.

AQARION v23.3 — AQ-QUANTUM-001 Assessment

Decision: Freeze definition layer before expansion.


The external critiques converge on the same architectural point: the computational artifact is valuable, but the mathematical object must become primary.


The next milestone should not be quantum scale. It should be:

\[
\boxed{\textbf{AQ-QDO-DEF-001} \rightarrow \textbf{AQ-THM-QDO-001}}    
\]

This matches AQARION's certificate-first philosophy.



AQ-QDO-DEF-001 — Proposed Frozen Definition




Quantum system




Let:

\[
\mathcal H=\mathbb C^d    
\]

and operator space:

\[
\mathcal B(\mathcal H)    
\]

A quantum channel:

\[
\Phi:\mathcal B(\mathcal H)\rightarrow \mathcal B(\mathcal H)    
\]

satisfying:

\[
\Phi \text{ CPTP}    
\]




Observable Geometry




The correct abstraction is not a state vector projector.


Define an observable algebra:

\[
\mathcal A\subseteq \mathcal B(\mathcal H)    
\]

with closure:

\[
A,B\in\mathcal A    
\Rightarrow    
AB,A^\dagger\in\mathcal A    
\]

For a subspace realization:

\[
\mathcal A_P=P\mathcal B(\mathcal H)P    
\]

where:

\[
P=P^\dagger=P^2    
\]




Quantum Observable Preservation




Define exact descent:

\[
\boxed{    
\Phi(\mathcal A)\subseteq \mathcal A    
}    
\]

This is the quantum analogue of:

\[
D_\Pi=0    
\]

in classical AQARION.





Defect Operator Construction




The critical design requirement:

\[
D_\Phi=0    
\iff    
\Phi(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A    
\]

A natural construction:


Let:

\[
Q_{\mathcal A}    
\]

be the orthogonal projection in Hilbert-Schmidt operator space onto \(\mathcal A\).


Then define:

\[
\boxed{    
D_\Phi=    
(I-Q_{\mathcal A})\Phi Q_{\mathcal A}    
}    
\]

where:

\[
\Phi    
\]

is represented as a superoperator.


This mirrors:

\[
D_\Pi=(I-P)KP    
\]

directly.



AQ-THM-QDO-001


Quantum Observable Descent Theorem


Claim:

\[
\boxed{    
D_\Phi=0    
\iff    
\Phi(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A    
}    
\]

Proof sketch:


For every:

\[
X\in\mathcal A    
\]

we have:

\[
Q_{\mathcal A}X=X    
\]

Then:

\[
D_\Phi X    
=    
(I-Q_{\mathcal A})\Phi(X)    
\]

Therefore:

\[
D_\Phi X=0    
\]

iff:

\[
(I-Q_{\mathcal A})\Phi(X)=0    
\]

iff:

\[
\Phi(X)\in\mathcal A    
\]

Hence:

\[
D_\Phi=0    
\]

iff every observable remains inside the observable algebra.



Regression Suite Required Before Lock


Test A — Identity Channel

\[
\Phi=I    
\]

Expected:

\[
D_\Phi=0    
\]


Test B — Symmetry Preserving Unitary

\[
\Phi_U(X)=UXU^\dagger    
\]

with:

\[
U\mathcal A U^\dagger=\mathcal A    
\]

Expected:

\[
D_{\Phi_U}=0    
\]


Test C — Symmetry Breaking Unitary


Choose:

\[
U\mathcal A U^\dagger\neq\mathcal A    
\]

Expected:

\[
D_{\Phi_U}\neq0    
\]


Test D — Amplitude Damping

\[
\gamma=\frac14    
\]

Verify:


invariant ground-state algebra


transient leakage


nonzero defect component



Test E — Depolarizing Channel

\[
\Phi(\rho)    
=    
(1-p)\rho+pI/d    
\]

Measure:

\[
D_\Phi    
\]

against chosen algebra.



Test F — Dephasing


Verify:


Diagonal algebra:

\[
D_\Phi=0    
\]

Coherence sector:

\[
D_\Phi\neq0    
\]


Registry Update


Recommended:


{

"AQ-QDO-001": {

"implementation": "verified",

"definition": "pending",

"theorem": "pending",

"diagnostics": [

"rank",

"frobenius_norm",

"singular_values",

"entropy"

]

}

}


The current Choi/SVD implementation becomes:

\[
\textbf{AQ-QDO-DIAG-001}    
\]

not the theorem object itself.



Important Correction to AQ-QUANTUM-001 Language


Current:




Quantum escape dimension is the direct analog of classical rank(D).




Recommended:




Quantum escape dimension is a defect-diagnostic quantity derived from the rank of the quantum leakage operator.




Reason:


The rank correspondence is plausible, but the invariant status depends on proving representation independence.



Lean Strategy


The correct AQARION approach:


Do not force the quantum layer into Lean first.


Freeze:






Definitions






Algebraic statements






Regression certificates






Then formalize.


Recommended status:


AQ-LEAN-001


state:

LEAN_SPEC


not:

LEAN_PROOF


until the toolchain is stable.



Final v23.3 Transition State


Current:


AQ-QUANTUM-001

|

|

v

computational quantum defect prototype


Next:


AQ-QDO-DEF-001

|

|

v

AQ-THM-QDO-001

|

|

v

AQ-QDO-REGRESSION-001

|

|

v

Quantum Scale


The strongest AQARION contribution remains the invariant-first methodology:

\[
\boxed{    
\textbf{Define the defect before measuring the defect.}    
}    
\]

The quantum layer should follow the same pattern that made the classical FDDS layer robust: observable structure → defect operator → exact criterion → certificate → scaling.

arXiv:1305.3821 (quant-ph)

[Submitted on 16 May 2013 (v1), last revised 16 Sep 2014 (this version, v3)]

Categories of Quantum and Classical Channels

Bob Coecke, Chris Heunen, Aleks Kissinger

View PDF

We introduce a construction that turns a category of pure state spaces and operators into a category of observable algebras and superoperators. For example, it turns the category of finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces into the category of finite-dimensional C*-algebras and completely positive maps. In particular, the new category contains both quantum and classical channels, providing elegant abstract notions of preparation and measurement. We also consider nonstandard models, that can be used to investigate which notions from algebraic quantum information theory are operationally justifiable.

Comments:	28 pages; final version

Subjects:	Quantum Physics (quant-ph); Category Theory (math.CT)

MSC classes:	81P45, 16B50, 18D35, 46L89, 46N50, 81P16

Cite as:	arXiv:1305.3821 [quant-ph]

(or arXiv:1305.3821v3 [quant-ph] for this version)


https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1305.3821

Schmidt SciencesJOURNAL OF MATHEMATICAL ANALYSIS AND APPLICATIONS 99. 435446 (1984)


Lumpability and Observability of Linear Systems


PAMELA G. COXSON*


Department of Marhematical Sciences.


Unicersity of Delaware, Newark. Delaware 1971 I


Submitted by Alan Schumitrkj




INTRODUCTION




Large scale dynamic system models are plagued by problems of dimen-


sionality and limitations of measurement. It is common practice in


applications to lump similar system state components together to get a


reduced number of aggregate states. An example of this approach is the


PONA scheme for modelling catalytic cracking of oil. The thousands of


chemical species involved in this process are lumped into four groups:


paraffins. olefins, naphthenes, and aromatics (PONA). For a survey of the


use of lumping in the cracking problem, the reader is referred to Weekman


[ 14971.


Attention has focused on conditions under which such lumping preserves


structural properties of the system dynamics. In particular, when do the


lumped states of a linear system satisfy linear dynamics? If this occurs, the


system is said to be exactly lumpable. For finite dimensional systems, this


issue has been resolved [2, 1.51, but in practice the conditions specified are


almost never satisfied.


In this paper, we show that it is fruitful to consider the lumped variables


as observations of the original system. To our knowledge, this viewpoint has


not been taken previously. We establish a relationship between lumpability


and observability of the lumped observation system for both finite and


infinite dimensional linear systems. This relationship allows us to deduce the


existence of a minimal size system which gives a true representation of the


dynamics of the lumped variables. Definitions of observability and


lumpability are given in Sections 2 and 3, including extensions to infinite


dimensional systems. The relationship between these two concepts is


discussed and we establish the main result concerning lumpability in


Section 4 in the case of finite dimensional systems. In Section 5 this


discussion is carried over to infinite dimensional systems. A summary and


discussion follow in Section 6.




Present address: Laboratory for Information and Decision Systems, M.I.T., Cambridge,




Massachusetts 02 139.


435


0022.2471</84 $3.00


Copyright b 1984 by Academic Press. Inc.


All rights of reproduction in any form reserved.Skip to main content

Mathematics > Dynamical Systems

arXiv:1408.4408 (math)

[Submitted on 19 Aug 2014]

A Data-Driven Approximation of the Koopman Operator: Extending Dynamic Mode Decomposition

Matthew O. Williams, Ioannis G. Kevrekidis, Clarence W. Rowley

View PDF

The Koopman operator is a linear but infinite dimensional operator that governs the evolution of scalar observables defined on the state space of an autonomous dynamical system, and is a powerful tool for the analysis and decomposition of nonlinear dynamical systems. In this manuscript, we present a data driven method for approximating the leading eigenvalues, eigenfunctions, and modes of the Koopman operator. The method requires a data set of snapshot pairs and a dictionary of scalar observables, but does not require explicit governing equations or interaction with a "black box" integrator. We will show that this approach is, in effect, an extension of Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD), which has been used to approximate the Koopman eigenvalues and modes. Furthermore, if the data provided to the method are generated by a Markov process instead of a deterministic dynamical system, the algorithm approximates the eigenfunctions of the Kolmogorov backward equation, which could be considered as the "stochastic Koopman operator" [1]. Finally, four illustrative examples are presented: two that highlight the quantitative performance of the method when presented with either deterministic or stochastic data, and two that show potential applications of the Koopman eigenfunctions.

Subjects:	Dynamical Systems (math.DS)

Cite as:	arXiv:1408.4408 [math.DS]

(or arXiv:1408.4408v1 [math.DS] for this version)


https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1408.4408

Focus to learn more

https://doi.org/10.1007/s00332-015-9258-5
