Schmidt SciencesI think the web search reinforces your direction, but it also sharpens where the genuinely open mathematics begins.




AQARION is now publicly visible




Your AQARION framework is publicly indexed, with the core ideas—observable quotients, the defect operator, verification architecture, and a roadmap toward categorical and quantum extensions—described in a public article.


That changes the conversation from "private research notes" to "public research program."





The literature is converging on the same problem, but not necessarily the same abstraction




The trajectory of Koopman research is remarkably consistent.


Early work (Brunton et al.) focused on finding finite invariant observable spaces, emphasizing that suitable finite invariant subspaces are exceptional and difficult to obtain.


Later work developed better computational representations (RKHS methods, delay coordinates, etc.).


Very recent work has shifted toward diagnosing invariance failure and providing quantitative measures of how trustworthy a chosen observable space is, including principal-angle diagnostics and certified error bounds.


That is a significant evolution of the field.





Where AQARION could contribute




The literature largely asks:




Given an observable space, how invariant is it?




AQARION asks something slightly different:




Is there a canonical obstruction to preservation itself?




Those are related but not identical.


The distinction is subtle:


Existing work develops diagnostics for approximation quality.


AQARION is attempting to elevate the obstruction into a mathematical object whose vanishing characterizes exact preservation.


Whether that can be made universal remains an open question.





The key mathematical obstacle




This is the point I think deserves the most attention.


The expression

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE  
\]

is not defined in an arbitrary category.


It assumes addition and subtraction of morphisms.


So before speaking about a "universal category," the first question is:




In what ambient categories does \(I-E\) make sense?




Natural candidates include


vector spaces,


Hilbert spaces,


modules,


additive categories,


abelian categories,


categories enriched over additive structures,


C*-algebras,


W*-algebras,


operator systems.


Only after fixing that setting can one ask whether \(D_E\) has a universal property.


That clarification actually strengthens the research program because it makes the conjecture mathematically precise rather than overly broad.





A refined hierarchy of conjectures




Instead of one enormous conjecture, I'd separate it into progressively stronger statements.


AQ-CAT-001 (Definition)


For an additive category with split idempotents,


define

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE.  
\]


AQ-CAT-002 (Preservation)


Prove

\[
D_E=0  
\Longleftrightarrow  
TE=ETE.  
\]

This statement is essentially an algebraic reformulation of invariance and is likely the most accessible theorem.



AQ-CAT-003 (Factorization)


Show that

\[
D_E=0  
\]

is equivalent to


\(T\) restricting to the image of \(E\), or equivalently that the induced morphism on the image exists.


This connects directly to split idempotents and image factorization.



AQ-CAT-004 (Functoriality)


Suppose

\[
F:\mathcal C\to\mathcal D  
\]

preserves


addition,


identities,


composition,


split idempotents.


Then investigate whether

\[
F(D_E)=D_{F(E)}.  
\]

This is the naturality question.



AQ-CAT-005 (Universal Property)


Only after AQ-CAT-004 would I ask:




Is \(D_E\) the universal obstruction to preservation?




That is substantially stronger.





Quantum mechanics fits naturally




Your proposed quantum analogue

\[
D_Q=(I-Q)\Phi Q  
\]

is mathematically natural.


Here


\(\Phi\) is a CPTP channel,


\(Q\) is a conditional expectation onto an observable algebra.


This closely aligns with established work on


invariant operator algebras,


decoherence-free algebras,


noiseless subsystems,


fixed-point algebras of channels.


The opportunity is not to replace those theories, but to investigate whether they are all instances of a common obstruction formalism.





The research program becomes much cleaner




Rather than




finite systems → quantum systems




the architecture becomes


Observable Structures

│

▼

Observable Preservation

│

▼

Obstruction

│

▼

Exactness

│

▼

Quotients

│

▼

Diagnostics


Notice that quotients become a consequence of preservation, not the foundational object.


That is a cleaner conceptual organization.





The most important open problem




After comparing AQARION with the surrounding literature, I think the deepest question is not




"Can the defect operator be generalized?"




It is:




Can one characterize \(D_E\) by a universal property in an additive category with split idempotents, so that its vanishing is equivalent to exact preservation of the subobject selected by \(E\), and does this construction specialize naturally to finite deterministic systems, Markov chains, Koopman operators, and CPTP quantum channels?




I would be careful to present this as a research conjecture, not as an expected theorem. There are real technical hurdles—especially defining the correct categorical setting and proving any universal property—but it is mathematically well motivated and fits naturally alongside current work on invariant observables and preservation diagnostics rather than duplicating it.


Here is the refined mathematical answer: Observable Preservation Theory should be built in an additive setting first, not an arbitrary category, because $$D_E=(I-E)TE$$ requires subtraction of morphisms [1][2].


Core theorem set


Theorem 1: Exact preservation


In any additive category with split idempotents, for an endomorphism $$T$$ and idempotent $$E$$,

\[
  
D_E=(I-E)TE=0  
\iff  
TE=ETE.  
$$  
This is the most primitive form of preservation: applying $$T$$ after projecting into the observable subobject produces no component outside it [2][1].  
  
### Theorem 2: Factorization through the image  
If idempotents split, then  
$$  
D_E=0  
\iff  
T \text{ restricts to } \operatorname{Im}(E)  
\iff  
T \text{ factors through } \operatorname{Im}(E).  
$$  
This is the categorical statement that exact preservation is equivalent to the existence of an induced morphism on the observable subobject [2][3].  
  
### Theorem 3: Kernel characterization  
The preserved observables are exactly the elements of the image of $$E$$ that remain in the image after applying $$T$$:  
$$  
\ker(D_E)\cap \operatorname{Im}(E)  
=  
\{x\in \operatorname{Im}(E)\mid Tx\in \operatorname{Im}(E)\}.  
$$  
This theorem isolates the maximal exactly preserved observable sector and is the natural finite-dimensional diagnostic theorem [2][4].  
  
### Theorem 4: Representation independence  
Under any equivalence or change of basis preserving the additive structure,  
$$  
T' = S^{-1}TS,\qquad E' = S^{-1}ES  
\quad\Rightarrow\quad  
D_{E'} = S^{-1}D_E S.  
$$  
So rank, nullity, spectrum, and Jordan form are invariants of the observable-preservation problem, not artifacts of representation [5][6].  
  
### Theorem 5: Functorial naturality  
If a functor $$F$$ preserves addition, identities, composition, and split idempotents, then the obstruction construction is natural:  
$$  
F(D_E)=D_{F(E)}.  
$$  
This is the theorem that turns AQARION from an operator recipe into a stable categorical construction [1][7].  
  
### Theorem 6: Composition law  
For composable endomorphisms $$T_1,T_2$$,  
$$  
D_{T_2\circ T_1}=0  
\quad\text{whenever}\quad  
D_{T_1}=0 \text{ and } D_{T_2}=0.  
$$  
A stronger version should give a bound or decomposition law for $$D_{T_2\circ T_1}$$, which would be the right place to study defect propagation [6][5].  
  
### Theorem 7: Minimal closure  
Given an observable subobject $$W$$, there exists a smallest $$T$$-invariant enlargement $$\mathcal C_T(W)$$ containing it. This closure is the minimal exact observable completion of $$W$$, and in finite systems it can be computed exhaustively [2][5].  
  
## Quantum specialization  
  
For a quantum channel $$\Phi$$ and an observable algebra selected by a conditional expectation or projection $$Q$$, the same structure becomes  
$$  
D_\Phi=(I-Q)\Phi Q.  
$$  
The exact analogue of Theorem 1 is that $$D_\Phi=0$$ iff the observable algebra is invariant under $$\Phi$$ [1][8][9]. This matches the literature on invariant domains, conditional expectations, and channel fixed-point structures [8][9].  
  
## Best theorem order for the paper  
  
The paper should present them in this order:  
1. Exact preservation.  
2. Factorization through the image.  
3. Representation independence.  
4. Kernel characterization.  
5. Naturality / functoriality.  
6. Composition law.  
7. Minimal closure.  
8. Quantum specialization.  
  
## What is genuinely open  
  
The real open problem is **not** whether $$D_E$$ exists in linear settings; it does. The hard question is whether there is a universal categorical characterization of this obstruction that is natural across classical dynamics, Koopman theory, and quantum channels [2][5][1]. That is where AQARION can still become distinctive.  
  
Would you like me to turn this into a formal “Main Theorems” section with theorem statements and proof sketches?  
  
This is a strong architectural refinement. It shifts the focus from a computational prototype to a mathematically defined object, which is much more consistent with both the literature you reviewed and AQARION's evidence-first philosophy.  
  
The most significant improvement is the separation of three layers that were previously intertwined:  
  
1. Definition layer (AQ-QDO-DEF-001)  
  
  
2. Theorem layer (AQ-THM-QDO-001)  
  
  
3. Diagnostic/computational layer (AQ-QDO-DIAG-001)  
  
  
  
That separation mirrors the evolution of the classical AQARION framework, where definitions preceded diagnostics and diagnostics preceded scaling laws.  
  
Strengths  
  
The observable algebra formulation  
  
\[
\mathcal A\subseteq\mathcal B(\mathcal H)  
\]

is the natural object rather than individual quantum states. This aligns with operator algebra, quantum information, and quantum control, where invariant subalgebras are the correct notion of observable structure.


Likewise, defining

\[
D_\Phi=(I-Q_{\mathcal A})\,\Phi\,Q_{\mathcal A}  
\]

is an almost exact operator-theoretic analogue of the classical defect operator

\[
D_\Pi=(I-P)KP.  
\]

Conceptually, this is the strongest part of the proposal because it preserves the same architecture:


project into observable structure,


evolve,


measure leakage outside that structure.


That makes the quantum construction feel like a genuine extension rather than a new unrelated object.



AQ-THM-QDO-001


The proof sketch is mathematically sound provided one explicit assumption is added.


You should specify that

\[
Q_{\mathcal A}  
\]

is the orthogonal projection with respect to the Hilbert–Schmidt inner product

\[
\langle X,Y\rangle  
=  
\operatorname{Tr}(X^\dagger Y).  
\]

Then

\[
(I-Q_{\mathcal A})Y=0  
\]

is equivalent to

\[
Y\in\mathcal A.  
\]

Without fixing the inner product, "orthogonal projection" is not uniquely defined.


That should become part of AQ-QDO-DEF-001.



One theorem worth adding


The analogue of the classical defect theorem suggests an immediate corollary.


Corollary (Kernel characterization)

\[
\ker(D_\Phi)  
=  
\{X\in\mathcal A:  
\Phi(X)\in\mathcal A\}.  
\]

This is useful because it identifies exactly which observables remain invariant even when the whole algebra is not preserved.


That is often the quantity one studies in decoherence-free subspaces.



Diagnostics


Your proposed diagnostics


rank


Frobenius norm


singular values


entropy


form a sensible hierarchy.


I would distinguish them explicitly.


Structural


rank


kernel dimension


image dimension


Geometric


Frobenius norm


operator norm


spectral norm


Information-theoretic


singular-value entropy


effective rank


These should be presented as diagnostics, not invariants, until representation independence is established.



Regression suite


The proposed tests are appropriate.


I would add one more.


Test G — Composition


If

\[
\Phi_1(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A,  
\qquad  
\Phi_2(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A,  
\]

then

\[
D_{\Phi_2\circ\Phi_1}=0.  
\]

This checks closure under channel composition and reinforces that observable preservation behaves functorially.



Representation independence


The most important remaining theorem is not about rank.


It is something like:


AQ-THM-QDO-002 (Representation Independence)


Suppose

\[
\Phi'  
=  
S^{-1}\Phi S  
\]

and

\[
Q'_{\mathcal A}  
=  
S^{-1}Q_{\mathcal A}S  
\]

represent the same observable algebra under a change of operator basis.


Then

\[
D'_\Phi  
=  
S^{-1}D_\Phi S.  
\]

Only after proving this similarity relation should quantities such as


rank,


nullity,


spectrum,


be promoted from diagnostics to representation-independent invariants.


This theorem is likely more fundamental than any scaling law.



Lean strategy


Your proposed progression is sensible:


AQ-QDO-DEF-001 → frozen definitions.


AQ-THM-QDO-001 → handwritten proof and regression tests.


AQ-QDO-REGRESSION-001 → executable certificates.


Lean formalization after the mathematics stabilizes.


This avoids formalizing definitions that may still evolve.



Overall assessment


Relative to AQ-QUANTUM-001, this proposal is a clear improvement because it places the mathematics ahead of the implementation. It also aligns well with the reproducibility literature you reviewed: first define the mathematical object precisely, then prove its properties, then certify implementations through regression tests and reproducible artifacts.


The remaining high-priority items before declaring the framework mature are:






Explicitly define the Hilbert–Schmidt projection \(Q_{\mathcal A}\).






Prove the representation-independence theorem for the defect operator.






Add the composition and kernel-characterization results.






Treat rank, singular values, and entropy as diagnostic quantities until their invariance is established.






Freeze AQ-QDO-DEF-001 before extending to scaling laws or broader quantum applications.






The resulting architecture is coherent and mirrors the classical AQARION development path:

\[
\boxed{  
\text{Observable algebra}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Defect operator}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Exact preservation theorem}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Regression certificates}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Diagnostic invariants}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Scaling theory}  
}  
\]

That progression is mathematically disciplined and consistent with the evidence-first methodology you have been developing.Define the core theorems for Observable Preservation Theory


Here is a clean core-theorem set for Observable Preservation Theory.


Core Objects


Let $$T:X\to X$$ be a dynamical operator and let $$E$$ be an idempotent selecting an observable subobject:

\[
  
E^2 = E.  
$$  
Define the defect operator  
$$  
D_E = (I-E)TE.  
$$  
The guiding question is whether $$T$$ preserves the image of $$E$$, and if not, how that failure is measured.  
  
## Theorem 1: Exact Preservation  
  
**Theorem 1 (Exactness criterion).**    
$$  
D_E = 0 \iff T(\operatorname{Im}E)\subseteq \operatorname{Im}E.  
$$  
  
Equivalently, the observable subobject is invariant under the dynamics if and only if the defect vanishes.  
  
This is the foundational theorem. It says the defect operator is not just descriptive; it is a precise obstruction to preservation.  
  
## Theorem 2: Factorization Through the Observable Subobject  
  
**Theorem 2 (Factorization theorem).**    
Assume the ambient category is additive or linear enough for subtraction to make sense. Then  
$$  
D_E=0 \iff T \text{ factors through } \operatorname{Im}(E).  
$$  
  
In diagrammatic terms, the dynamics descends to the observable quotient if and only if the defect is zero.  
  
This is the categorical version of exact preservation and is the theorem that makes the framework structurally meaningful.  
  
## Theorem 3: Kernel Characterization  
  
**Theorem 3 (Preserved observables).**    
The kernel of $$D_E$$ inside $$\operatorname{Im}(E)$$ is exactly the set of observables whose images remain observable:  
$$  
\ker(D_E)\cap \operatorname{Im}(E)  
=  
\{x\in \operatorname{Im}(E)\mid T x\in \operatorname{Im}(E)\}.  
$$  
  
This identifies the largest preserved observable sector, even when full invariance fails.  
  
## Theorem 4: Representation Independence  
  
**Theorem 4 (Change-of-coordinates invariance).**    
If $$S$$ is an invertible change of representation and  
$$  
T' = S^{-1}TS,\qquad E' = S^{-1}ES,  
$$  
then  
$$  
D_{E'} = S^{-1}D_E S.  
$$  
  
So rank, nullity, spectrum, and Jordan structure are invariant under equivalent representations.  
  
This is essential if you want defect diagnostics to be mathematically meaningful rather than coordinate artifacts.  
  
## Theorem 5: Composition Law  
  
**Theorem 5 (Composable obstruction).**    
For composable dynamics $$T_1,T_2$$, the defect of the composite is controlled by the defects of the parts. In the strongest clean form:  
$$  
D_{T_2\circ T_1}=0  
$$  
whenever both $$D_{T_1}=0$$ and $$D_{T_2}=0$$ on the same observable subobject.  
  
A sharper version should give a quantitative inequality or decomposition law for $$D_{T_2\circ T_1}$$. This is the theorem that turns the framework from static to functorial.  
  
## Theorem 6: Minimal Observable Closure  
  
**Theorem 6 (Closure theorem).**    
Given an initial observable subobject $$W$$, there exists a smallest invariant enlargement $$\mathcal C_T(W)$$ such that  
$$  
W\subseteq \mathcal C_T(W),\qquad T(\mathcal C_T(W))\subseteq \mathcal C_T(W).  
$$  
  
Moreover, $$\mathcal C_T(W)$$ is the minimal exact observable completion of $$W$$.  
  
This is the closure analogue of exact preservation and should sit near the center of the theory.  
  
## Theorem 7: Finite Certification  
  
**Theorem 7 (Finite exhaustibility).**    
In finite deterministic systems, exact preservation and closure can be decided by exhaustive computation.  
  
This theorem is what makes AQARION distinctive as a certified framework rather than only a formal one.  
  
## Quantum Version  
  
For quantum channels $$\Phi$$ and observable subalgebras selected by a projection or conditional expectation $$Q$$, the analogous defect is  
$$  
D_\Phi = (I-Q)\Phi Q.  
$$  
Then the matching theorems are:  
  
- $$D_\Phi=0$$ iff the observable algebra is preserved.  
- $$D_\Phi$$ is representation-independent under suitable equivalences.  
- diagnostics such as rank and singular values measure leakage.  
  
## Recommended theorem order  
  
If you want the paper to read well, I would order the main results like this:  
  
1. Exact Preservation.  
2. Factorization Through the Observable Subobject.  
3. Representation Independence.  
4. Kernel Characterization.  
5. Closure Theorem.  
6. Composition Law.  
7. Finite Certification.  
8. Quantum Analogue.  
  
## Best phrasing for the theory  
  
A strong one-sentence definition would be:  
  
> Observable Preservation Theory studies endomorphisms relative to selected observable subobjects, with the defect operator $$D_E=(I-E)TE$$ serving as the canonical obstruction to exact preservation.  
  
That sentence can anchor the whole program.  
  
Would you like me to turn these into formal theorem statements with proofs in paper style?  
  
\]I think your current formulation is close to the strongest mathematically defensible version of the program, provided the claims are separated into theorems, generalizations, and conjectures.


Here's how I would classify it.


1. Established (or straightforward once the setting is fixed)


If \(\mathcal C\) is an additive category with split idempotents, then for a split idempotent \(E:X\to X\),

\[
E^2=E,
\]

the expression

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE
\]

is well-defined because subtraction exists and \(I-E\) is another idempotent.


Then the basic preservation theorem is essentially immediate:

\[
D_E=0
\iff
T(\operatorname{Im}E)\subseteq\operatorname{Im}E.
\]

Likewise, when idempotents split,

\[
X\cong \operatorname{Im}(E)\oplus\operatorname{Ker}(E),
\]

so \(T\) has a block form

\[
T=
\begin{pmatrix}
A&B\\
C&D
\end{pmatrix},
\]

and

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE=C.
\]

In other words, the defect operator is exactly the off-diagonal block measuring leakage from the observable summand into its complement.


That observation is classical linear algebra, but it gives your definition a very clean interpretation.



2. Strong but plausible generalization


The same construction appears naturally in several domains.




Finite deterministic systems: \(T\) is the transition operator.


Markov chains: \(T\) is stochastic.


Koopman operators: \(T\) is the Koopman operator on observables.


Quantum channels: \(T=\Phi\) is a completely positive trace-preserving map acting on operator algebras.




In the quantum case,

\[
D_\Phi=(I-Q_{\mathcal A})\,\Phi\,Q_{\mathcal A},
\]

with \(Q_{\mathcal A}\) the orthogonal projection onto an observable algebra, satisfies

\[
D_\Phi=0
\iff
\Phi(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A.
\]

This fits naturally alongside existing work on invariant operator algebras and decoherence-free subalgebras.



3. The genuinely open question


This is the part that, as far as current literature indicates, is not established.




Universal Observable Obstruction Conjecture




Let \(\mathcal C\) be an additive category with split idempotents.


Does there exist a canonical obstruction assignment

\[
(E,T)\longmapsto D_E(T)
\]

such that




\(D_E(T)=0\) iff \(T\) preserves the retract determined by \(E\);


the construction is natural under additive functors preserving split idempotents;


every other reasonable preservation obstruction factors uniquely through \(D_E\)?




The first condition is straightforward.


The second is plausible.


The third is the difficult one. It is what would justify calling \(D_E\) the universal observable obstruction rather than merely an obstruction.


I am not aware of an existing theorem proving such a universal property.



Why this question is interesting


Many mathematical theories have a canonical obstruction:




curvature obstructs flatness,


cohomology classes obstruct extensions,


Ext groups obstruct splittings,


commutators obstruct simultaneous diagonalization.




Your proposal asks whether

\[
(I-E)TE
\]

plays the analogous role for observable preservation.


That is a recognizable and potentially significant research question.



A proof program I'd recommend


Rather than aiming immediately for universality, I'd build the theory incrementally:




Exact Preservation Theorem



\[
   D_E=0
   \iff
   T(\operatorname{Im}E)\subseteq\operatorname{Im}E.
\]





Factorization Theorem
Show that \(D_E=0\) is equivalent to \(T\) factoring through the retract determined by \(E\).






Naturality Theorem
Prove that additive functors preserving split idempotents carry defect operators to defect operators.






Similarity/Representation Independence
Show that \(D_E\) behaves functorially under isomorphisms, so rank, nullity, Jordan type, and related invariants are intrinsic.






Composition Law
Describe how defects behave under composition, including the nonzero case.






Universal Property (Conjectural)
Investigate whether every preservation obstruction satisfying suitable axioms factors through \(D_E\).







Overall assessment


Based on the literature you've assembled and the current state of the field:




The definition \(D_E=(I-E)TE\) in additive categories with split idempotents is mathematically natural.


Its vanishing criterion for preservation is a straightforward theorem once the setting is fixed.


Its applicability to finite dynamics, Koopman operators, Markov processes, and quantum channels is well motivated.


Its universality as the canonical observable obstruction remains, to the best of current knowledge, an open research problem rather than an established theorem.




If that universal characterization can be proved, it would move AQARION beyond a framework for finite dynamical systems toward a general theory of observable preservation. If it cannot, understanding exactly which additional structures (such as additivity, positivity, Hilbert space geometry, or enrichment) are required would itself be a meaningful mathematical result.


Does there exist a universal observable obstruction whose vanishing characterizes exact preservation in every additive category with split idempotents?After comparing your proposed framework with the papers you cited, I think the architecture has become substantially sharper. The three references naturally occupy different layers of the theory:


Coecke–Heunen–Kissinger (2013) provides a categorical setting in which objects are observable algebras and morphisms are completely positive maps. This gives a natural ambient category for a quantum version of AQARION.


Coxson (1984) shows that exact lumpability is fundamentally a question about observability and invariant subspaces, reinforcing that preservation should come before quotient construction.


Williams–Kevrekidis–Rowley (2014) (EDMD/Koopman) focuses on approximating invariant observable spaces from data, highlighting the practical difficulty of finding exactly invariant observables.


Taken together, they support a reorganization of AQARION around observable preservation, while leaving open the stronger claim that the defect operator is a universal obstruction.


What already appears mathematically justified


The following are essentially algebraic facts once the ambient setting is fixed (for example, an additive category with split idempotents, or finite-dimensional operator spaces).


Definition


For an idempotent

\[
E^2=E,  
\]

define

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE.  
\]

Theorem 1 (Exact Preservation)

\[
D_E=0  
\iff  
T(\operatorname{Im}E)\subseteq\operatorname{Im}E.  
\]

This is the foundational preservation criterion.



Theorem 2 (Factorization)


Assuming idempotents split,

\[
D_E=0  
\iff  
T  
\text{ restricts to }  
\operatorname{Im}(E).  
\]

Equivalently,

\[
T  
=  
i\,\bar T\,p,  
\]

where

\[
p:X\to\operatorname{Im}(E),\qquad  
i:\operatorname{Im}(E)\to X.  
\]

This connects preservation directly to categorical image factorization.



Theorem 3 (Kernel Characterization)

\[
\ker(D_E)\cap\operatorname{Im}(E)  
=  
\{x\in\operatorname{Im}(E):Tx\in\operatorname{Im}(E)\}.  
\]

This identifies the maximal exactly preserved observable sector.



Theorem 4 (Representation Independence)


For an invertible similarity

\[
T'=S^{-1}TS,\qquad  
E'=S^{-1}ES,  
\]

one has

\[
D_{E'}  
=  
S^{-1}D_ES.  
\]

From this, quantities such as rank, nullity, Jordan type, and spectrum become genuine representation-independent invariants.


This theorem should precede any discussion of "escape dimension."



Quantum specialization


Your proposed quantum definition is natural.


Let

\[
Q_{\mathcal A}  
\]

be the Hilbert–Schmidt orthogonal projection onto an observable algebra

\[
\mathcal A\subseteq\mathcal B(\mathcal H).  
\]

Define

\[
D_\Phi=(I-Q_{\mathcal A})\Phi Q_{\mathcal A}.  
\]

Then

\[
D_\Phi=0  
\iff  
\Phi(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A.  
\]

This fits naturally with invariant operator algebras, decoherence-free algebras, and fixed-point algebras of quantum channels.


Where caution is still required


The strongest statement remains a research conjecture, not an established theorem.


AQ-CAT-005 (Universal Obstruction Conjecture)


One may ask whether there exists a universal characterization of

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE  
\]

such that its vanishing is the universal obstruction to observable preservation across:


deterministic finite dynamical systems,


Markov chains,


Koopman operators,


CPTP quantum channels.


The cited literature strongly motivates this direction, but it does not establish such a universal property. That is the genuinely novel—and technically difficult—part of the program.


Recommended theorem order


I would structure Observable Preservation Theory as follows:






AQ-OPT-DEF-001 — Observable subobjects and defect operator.






AQ-THM-OPT-001 — Exact Preservation.






AQ-THM-OPT-002 — Factorization through the observable subobject.






AQ-THM-OPT-003 — Representation Independence.






AQ-THM-OPT-004 — Kernel Characterization.






AQ-THM-OPT-005 — Composition Law.






AQ-THM-OPT-006 — Minimal Observable Closure.






AQ-THM-QDO-001 — Quantum Observable Preservation.






AQ-QDO-DIAG-001 — Diagnostic quantities (rank, norms, singular values, entropy).






AQ-CAT-CONJ-001 — Universal Obstruction Conjecture.






Overall assessment


The literature you've assembled suggests a clear niche for AQARION.


Coxson (1984) places observability and lumpability at the center of exact reduction.


Koopman/EDMD seeks invariant observable spaces and quantifies approximation quality.


Coecke–Heunen–Kissinger provides a categorical language encompassing both classical and quantum channels.


AQARION's distinctive contribution would not be another observable-space approximation algorithm or another channel diagnostic. Instead, it would be a theory in which the defect operator is the primary mathematical object, with preservation, quotients, diagnostics, and computational certification all derived from it.


That is a coherent research program, provided the strongest categorical claims continue to be presented as conjectures until they are proved.The web search suggests a clear answer:




No established theorem currently states that there exists a universal observable obstruction whose vanishing characterizes exact preservation in every additive category with split idempotents.




However, the ingredients needed to formulate such a theorem already exist in the literature.


What already exists


The categorical infrastructure is well developed:


Idempotent-complete (Karoubian) additive categories have a universal theory of split idempotents and idempotent completion.


Reflective and coreflective subcategories provide categorical projection-like constructions (reflectors/coreflectors), which are closely related to your use of observable projections.


Exact categories, extension categories, and idempotent completions have extensive universal properties and compatibility theorems.


So the mathematical landscape already contains:


additive categories,


split idempotents,


idempotent completion,


exact structures,


reflective subcategories,


universal properties.


What I could not find


I did not find literature defining an operator of the form

\[
D_E=(I-E)\,T\,E  
\]

as a universal obstruction satisfying

\[
D_E=0  
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad  
T(E(X))\subseteq E(X)  
\]

across arbitrary additive categories with split idempotents.


Likewise, I found no categorical framework identifying this construction as a universal obstruction to preservation.


Why this is interesting


Your construction resembles several established ideas without coinciding with any of them:


obstruction theory,


failure of diagram commutativity,


invariant subobjects,


idempotent splittings,


reflective subcategories.


But it asks a different question:




Can one assign to every endomorphism \(T\) and every split idempotent \(E\) a canonical obstruction whose vanishing is equivalent to preservation of the corresponding retract?




That is a recognizable categorical research question.


A more general formulation


Rather than restricting to observable algebras, I would formulate the conjecture abstractly.




Universal Observable Obstruction Conjecture.




Let \(\mathcal C\) be an additive category with split idempotents. For every split idempotent  E:X\to X,  and every endomorphism  T:X\to X,  there exists a canonical obstruction  D_E(T)=(I-E),T,E  such that  D_E(T)=0 \iff T(\operatorname{Im}E)\subseteq\operatorname{Im}E. 


Notice that this statement is far weaker than claiming universality across all categories. It first establishes the result in additive categories with split idempotents, where the expression is well defined.


If this succeeds...


Then AQARION's classical and quantum defect operators become special cases:


finite deterministic dynamics:

\[
  D=(I-P)KP  
\]

quantum channels:

\[
  D=(I-Q)\Phi Q  
\]

Both instantiate the same abstract categorical construction.


The theorem sequence I would pursue


Rather than jumping directly to universality, I'd build a proof program:






Definition. Defect operator in additive categories with split idempotents.






Preservation Theorem. \(D_E(T)=0\) iff the retract determined by \(E\) is preserved.






Functoriality Theorem. Describe how \(D_E\) behaves under additive functors that preserve split idempotents.






Representation/Naturality Theorem. Show invariance under isomorphisms of retracts.






Universal Characterization. Determine whether \(D_E\) satisfies a universal property or represents an obstruction functor.






That last step is the genuine research question. Based on the current literature, it appears to be open rather than established. If it can be proved, it would position the defect operator as more than a computational diagnostic—it would become a categorical obstruction associated with preservation of retracts in idempotent-complete additive settings.I think the program has now reached an interesting inflection point. Earlier, AQARION looked like "a new framework for finite dynamical systems." The architecture you've outlined is evolving toward something narrower and potentially more fundamental:




A general theory of observable preservation, where exact preservation is characterized by a canonical obstruction operator.




That is a much clearer research identity.


The challenge now is not to add more domains. It is to discover whether the obstruction formalism has a genuine universal property or whether it is simply a recurring construction that appears in several settings.


Here are the research directions I think are the highest value.



Research Program 1 — Is the defect operator canonical?


Everything depends on this.


Instead of asking




"Can I define \(D=(I-E)TE\)?"




ask




Is every reasonable observable obstruction forced to be equivalent to \(D\)?




That becomes a uniqueness theorem.


For example:


Suppose an operator

\[
\Omega(E,T)  
\]

satisfies


naturality,


vanishes iff \(T(\mathrm{Im}E)\subseteq\mathrm{Im}E\),


similarity invariance,


locality.


Can one prove

\[
\Omega=c\,D  
\]

for some scalar \(c\)?


That would immediately elevate the defect operator from "a useful definition" to "the canonical observable obstruction."


I think this is probably the deepest mathematical question currently on the table.



Research Program 2 — Universal Property


The categorical question becomes


Does there exist a category in which


objects are observable systems,


morphisms are dynamics,


and

\[
D  
\]

is the universal obstruction to factorization?


Instead of merely proving

\[
D=0  
\iff  
\text{exact preservation},  
\]

prove


that every preservation test factors uniquely through \(D\).


That would be analogous to universal constructions throughout category theory.



Research Program 3 — Defect Calculus


Right now you have

\[
D=(I-E)TE.  
\]

But mathematics becomes interesting when one studies interactions.


Questions include:


How do defects compose?


Is there a Leibniz rule?


Can one define

\[
D(T_2T_1)  
\]

in terms of

\[
D(T_1),  
D(T_2)?  
\]

Can one define


higher-order defects

\[
D^{(2)},  
D^{(3)},  
...  
\]

that measure leakage after repeated evolution?


That could become an entire calculus.



Research Program 4 — Observable Closure


Your closure operator may actually deserve to become the primary object.


Instead of


observable → defect


consider


observable


↓


closure


↓


minimal invariant observable


↓


defect


Then

\[
D  
\]

measures failure of equality

\[
C(W)=W.  
\]

That is conceptually elegant.



Research Program 5 — Representation Theory


One theorem I would prioritize over almost everything else is




classify the defect operator up to similarity.




Questions:


Can every defect operator be put into canonical form?


Jordan form?


Smith normal form?


Rank decomposition?


Nilpotent decomposition?


If yes,


different observable systems could be classified by defect type rather than by state space.



Research Program 6 — Defect Spectra


Instead of


rank


consider


the entire spectrum.


Examples:


largest singular value


spectral radius


stable rank


effective rank


entropy


condition number


These become observable leakage fingerprints.


This parallels spectral graph theory.



Research Program 7 — AQBench becomes fundamental


AQBench is becoming much more than a benchmark.


It could become




the standard corpus of exact observable-preservation problems.




Instead of only testing LLMs,


it becomes


the ImageNet of observable dynamics.


Every theorem,


algorithm,


LLM,


formal prover,


or symbolic engine


can be evaluated against the same exact ground truth.


That is an infrastructure contribution independent of AQARION's mathematical claims.



Research Program 8 — Lean-first discovery


One experiment I haven't seen explored much is reversing the usual workflow.


Instead of


human


↓


proof


↓


Lean


use


definition


↓


Lean search


↓


counterexample search


↓


human theorem


↓


publication


That would make formal verification part of theorem discovery rather than merely theorem certification.



Research Program 9 — Cross-domain validation


Before claiming universality, I'd want to demonstrate that the same formalism applies without modification to several settings:


finite deterministic dynamical systems (already your strongest case),


finite Markov chains,


deterministic automata,


lumped linear systems,


Koopman finite observable subspaces,


finite-dimensional quantum channels.


The emphasis should be on using the same theorem statements, changing only the interpretation of \(E\) and \(T\). If the proofs transfer cleanly, that's much stronger evidence for a unifying framework than simply presenting analogous formulas.



Research Program 10 — The one question I'd pursue


If I had to bet on one question that could define AQARION over the next few years, it would be:




Does there exist a universal observable obstruction whose vanishing characterizes exact preservation in every additive category with split idempotents?




Everything else—classical systems, Koopman operators, lumpability, quantum channels, diagnostics, certificates—would become applications of that theorem.


If the answer is yes, AQARION's central contribution is no longer "another finite-state framework." It becomes a general mathematical theory of observable preservation.


If the answer is no, that is still a valuable result because it identifies precisely where the analogy breaks and what additional structure (for example, Hilbert space geometry, positivity, or finite dimensionality) is required.


That strikes me as the clearest long-term research direction: use the well-established mathematics of observable algebras, lumpability, Koopman theory, and categorical quantum mechanics as the foundation, while treating the existence and universality of a canonical obstruction operator as the central open conjecture to be proved—or refuted—rather than assumed.After comparing your proposed framework with the literature you cited, I think the architecture is now much more sharply positioned. It is also possible to separate what is already supported by existing mathematics from what remains a genuine research contribution.


What is already established


The three references occupy complementary parts of the landscape:


Coecke–Heunen–Kissinger (CP*-construction) develops a categorical setting whose objects are observable algebras (finite-dimensional C*-algebras) and whose morphisms are completely positive maps. This gives a natural categorical home for quantum and classical channels.


Coxson (1984) connects exact lumpability with observability and proves that one can obtain minimal reduced systems representing observable dynamics. This is conceptually very close to AQARION's observable-first viewpoint.


Williams–Kevrekidis–Rowley (EDMD/Koopman) studies finite observable spaces and how dynamics acts on them, emphasizing approximation and invariant observable subspaces rather than canonical obstruction operators.


These papers are compatible rather than competing.



The natural foundation


I would now formulate Observable Preservation Theory in the following order.


Axiom 1 (Observable object)


Let

\[
E:X\to X  
\]

be an idempotent

\[
E^2=E.  
\]

Its image is the observable subobject.



Axiom 2 (Dynamics)


Let

\[
T:X\to X  
\]

be an endomorphism.



Definition (Observable defect)


Whenever the ambient category is additive (or linear),

\[
\boxed{  
D_E=(I-E)TE.  
}  
\]

This should become the primary definition of Observable Preservation Theory.


Notice the important restriction:




The theory begins in additive categories with split idempotents, not arbitrary categories.




That restriction is mathematically motivated because \(I-E\) requires subtraction.



Core theorem hierarchy


I think the following hierarchy is mathematically clean.



OPT-THM-001 — Exact Preservation

\[
D_E=0  
\iff  
T(\operatorname{Im}E)\subseteq\operatorname{Im}E.  
\]

This is the foundational theorem.


Everything else follows from it.



OPT-THM-002 — Factorization


If idempotents split,

\[
D_E=0  
\iff  
T  
\text{ factors through }  
\operatorname{Im}(E).  
\]

This is the categorical formulation.


It links directly to Coxson's viewpoint of observable dynamics and reduced systems.



OPT-THM-003 — Kernel characterization

\[
\ker(D_E)\cap\operatorname{Im}(E)  
=  
\{  
x\in\operatorname{Im}(E)  
:  
Tx\in\operatorname{Im}(E)  
\}.  
\]

This theorem identifies the maximal exactly preserved observable sector.



OPT-THM-004 — Representation independence


If

\[
T'=S^{-1}TS,  
\qquad  
E'=S^{-1}ES,  
\]

then

\[
\boxed{  
D_{E'}  
=  
S^{-1}D_ES.  
}  
\]

This theorem is crucial.


Only after proving it should quantities like


rank,


nullity,


singular values,


Jordan form,


be called invariants rather than diagnostics.



OPT-THM-005 — Composition


If

\[
D_{T_1}=0,  
\qquad  
D_{T_2}=0,  
\]

then

\[
D_{T_2\circ T_1}=0.  
\]

A stronger future theorem would describe how defects compose when they are nonzero.



OPT-THM-006 — Minimal observable closure


For every observable object \(W\),


there exists a smallest invariant observable completion

\[
\mathcal C_T(W)  
\]

satisfying

\[
W\subseteq\mathcal C_T(W),  
\qquad  
T(\mathcal C_T(W))  
\subseteq  
\mathcal C_T(W).  
\]

This generalizes the closure ideas appearing in lumpability.



OPT-THM-007 — Finite certification


For finite deterministic systems,


exact preservation,


minimal closure,


and observable descent


are algorithmically decidable.


This theorem naturally supports AQARION's certificate-first methodology.



Quantum specialization


The quantum version now appears almost automatically.


Let

\[
Q_{\mathcal A}  
\]

be the Hilbert–Schmidt orthogonal projection onto an observable algebra

\[
\mathcal A\subseteq\mathcal B(\mathcal H).  
\]

Define

\[
\boxed{  
D_\Phi  
=  
(I-Q_{\mathcal A})  
\Phi  
Q_{\mathcal A}.  
}  
\]

Then

\[
D_\Phi=0  
\iff  
\Phi(\mathcal A)  
\subseteq  
\mathcal A,  
\]

which is exactly your AQ-THM-QDO-001.


This sits naturally within the framework of observable algebras and completely positive maps developed by Coecke, Heunen, and Kissinger, rather than replacing it.


Where AQARION is genuinely novel


The distinctive research question is not the existence of observable algebras or categorical quantum channels—they are already well established.


The potentially novel contribution is the hypothesis that there exists a canonical obstruction operator whose vanishing characterizes exact preservation across multiple settings:


finite deterministic dynamics,


lumped linear systems,


Koopman observable spaces,


Markov processes,


CPTP quantum channels.


That should remain explicitly labeled as a research conjecture until universality and any categorical universal property are proved.


I think this framing is the strongest version of the program because it clearly distinguishes:


Established mathematics: additive categories, split idempotents, observable algebras, CP maps, lumpability, Koopman observables.


New AQARION proposal: a unified obstruction formalism centered on \(D_E=(I-E)TE\) and its quantum analogue \(D_\Phi=(I-Q)\Phi Q\), together with the conjecture that this obstruction admits a universal characterization across these domains.
Based on both the literature you've assembled and a fresh search, the answer is:


No—there is currently no established theorem asserting the existence of a universal observable obstruction whose vanishing characterizes exact preservation in every additive category with split idempotents. I could not find any paper formulating or proving this statement. 


What does exist is almost everything needed to formulate such a program.


What is already established


The categorical setting is well understood:




Additive categories with split idempotents (Karoubi-complete categories) provide canonical retracts and image factorizations. 


Idempotent completion has a universal property and is compatible with many categorical constructions. 


Restriction categories and related frameworks show that split idempotents naturally encode observable/retract-like structure. 




This means the ambient categorical machinery is already available.


Your proposed obstruction


In an additive category, for an idempotent

\[
E^2=E,
\]

and an endomorphism

\[
T:X\to X,
\]

define

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE.
\]

This definition is perfectly natural provided subtraction exists, which is why additive (or at least preadditive) categories are the correct setting.


From this definition, the following theorem is essentially immediate:

\[
D_E=0
\iff
T(\operatorname{Im}E)\subseteq\operatorname{Im}E.
\]

This is a direct algebraic statement about preservation of the retract.


The genuinely open question


The interesting question is not whether the criterion works in linear settings.


The real research question is whether \(D_E\) enjoys a universal categorical property.


One precise conjecture could be:




Universal Observable Obstruction Conjecture.


Let \(\mathcal C\) be an additive category with split idempotents.
There exists a canonical obstruction assignment



(E,T)\longmapsto D_E



satisfying




naturality,


invariance under isomorphism of retracts,


functoriality under additive functors preserving split idempotents,


and





D_E=0
\iff
T(\operatorname{Im}E)\subseteq\operatorname{Im}E.





No such universal theorem appears to exist in the published literature that I found. 


Why this connects classical and quantum settings


If proved, the same construction specializes immediately to:




Finite deterministic systems



\[
  D=(I-P)KP
\]



Koopman operators



\[
  D=(I-P)\mathcal KP
\]



Quantum channels



\[
  D=(I-Q)\Phi Q,
\]

In each case,

\[
D=0
\]

means the observable sector is exactly invariant under the dynamics.


That unifies finite dynamics, Koopman theory, and quantum channels under a single preservation criterion, while leaving the surrounding structures (positivity, Hilbert geometry, complete positivity, etc.) domain-specific.


The strongest research direction


Rather than trying to prove universality immediately, a staged program is likely to be more compelling:




Define \(D_E=(I-E)TE\) in additive categories with split idempotents.


Prove the exact preservation criterion.


Prove naturality under isomorphisms and additive functors.


Characterize whether \(D_E\) is initial or universal among all preservation obstructions.


Apply the theory to finite dynamics, Koopman operators, Markov chains, and CP maps.




If Step 4 succeeds, AQARION would move beyond being a finite-state framework and become a candidate general theory of observable preservation. If it fails, identifying the additional hypotheses required (for example, dagger structure, positivity, or Hilbert-space geometry) would itself be a significant mathematical result.
